fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Benign epithelial pedunculated or sessile proliferations that most often occur on skin and can spread by auto-inoculation are called {{c1::verruca vulgaris}}
fields.Extra: HPV 2+++
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Verruca vulgaris are most commonly caused by which strain of HPV ? {{c1::HPV 2}}
fields.Extra: HPV 2+++
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Condyloma acuminatum is cause by which strains of HPV ? {{c1::HPV 6 and 11}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: HPV infection that manifests as genital warts that may also appear intraorally are called {{c1::condyloma acuminatum}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Multiple small, dome-shaped warts on oral mucosa caused by HPV 13 and 32 are called {{c1::Focal Epithelial Hyperplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Focal epithelial hyperplasia is also called {{c1::Heck's disease}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Focal epithelial hyperplasia or Heck's Disease is caused by which strains of HPV ? {{c1::HPV 13 and 32}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Oral hairy leukoplakia is caused by which virus ? {{c1::Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A white patch on the lateral tongue that does not wipe off due to an opportunistic EBV infection is called {{c1::oral hairy leukoplakia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which EBV related lesion can be the sign of progression from HIV to AIDS ? {{c1::Oral hairy leukoplakia}}
fields.Extra: Can also be found in other highly immunocompromised patients, but if HIV status unknown, this lesion is an indication for HIV antibody test.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Oral hairy leukoplakia is associated with what type of lymphoma ? {{c1::Burkitt's lymphoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which disease is caused by contact with the bacteria Treponema pallidum ? {{c1::Syphilis}}
fields.Extra: Source: CDC, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Syphilis is caused by contact with which bacteria ? {{c1::Treponema pallidum}}
fields.Extra: Source: CDC, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the primary lesion of Syphilis ? {{c1::Chancre}}
fields.Extra: Source: CDC, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the secondary lesions of Syphilis ? - {{c1::Oral mucous patch}} - {{c2::Condyloma latum}} - {{c3::Maculopapular rash}}
fields.Extra: Source: CDC, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the tertiary lesions of Syphilis ? - {{c1::Gumma (form of granuloma)}} - {{c1::CNS involvement}} - {{c1::Cardiovascular involvement}}
fields.Extra: Source: Wellcome Images, CC BY-SA 4.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the pathognomonic diagnostic features of Congenital Syphilis (Hutchinson's triad) ? - {{c1::Hutchinson teeth (notched incisors/mulberry molars)}} - {{c1::Ocular keratitis}} - {{c1::Deafness (VIII nerve)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Tuberculosis is caused by inhalation of which bacteria ? {{c1::Mycobacterium tuberculosis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Oral nonhealing chronic ulcers following lung infection are highly evocative of what disease ? {{c1::Tuberculosis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Primary tuberculosis infection of the {{c1:: hilar }} lymph node and may show up on an x-ray as a {{c2:: Ghon complex }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Mycobacterium tuberculosis surrounded by granuloma undergoing caseating necrosis is called the {{c1::Ghon complex}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Secondary tuberculosis infection leads to more widespread lung infection and {{c1::cavitation}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: After secondary tuberculosis , the infection becomes more widespread and this stage is called {{c1::miliary}}
fields.Extra: Diffuse disemmination through the vascular system that resemble millet seeds --> miliary
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A mild-to-moderate sore throat, diffuse erythema and scattered pustules are common signs of what sexually transmitted disease ? {{c1::Gonorrhea}}
fields.Extra: - STD (most commonly through fellatio) --> cervix and urethra are most common primary site of involvement - May present as similar to necrotizing gingivitis without the fetor oris
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gonorrhea is caused by which bacteria ? {{c1::Neisseria gonorrhoeae}}
fields.Extra: - STD (most commonly through fellatio) --> cervix and urethra are most common primary site of involvement - May present as similar to necrotizing gingivitis without the fetor oris
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Actinomycosis is caused by which bacteria ? {{c1::Actinomyces israelii}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the two classic types of actinomycosis ? - {{c1::Periapical}} - {{c1::Cervicofacial}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What typical finding is present in the purulent exudate of actinomycosis infection ? {{c1::sulfur granules}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Scarlet fever is caused by which bacteria ? {{c1::Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When strep throat becomes a systemic infection it is called {{c1::Scarlet Fever}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Scarlet fever is characterized by a white-coated tongue with inflamed fungiform papillae nicknamed {{c1::strawberry tongue}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The classic "strawberry tongue" finding in scarlet fever is a {{c1::white::color}}-coated tongue with inflamed {{c2::fungiform}} papillae
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A white plaque that rubs off should be assumed to be what pathology ? {{c1::pseudomembranous candidiasis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The candida-associated lesion of the dorsum of the tongue is called {{c1::median rhomboid glossitis}}
fields.Extra: = posterior midline atrophic candidiasis
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The candida-associated lesion of the corner of the mouth is called {{c1::angular cheilitis}}
fields.Extra: Often found in denture patients with underevaluated VDO Source: Matthew Ferguson 57, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Aphtous ulcers appear on which type of epithelium ? {{c1::Nonkeratinized}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Minor aphtous ulcers heal {{c1::without::with/without}} scarring and are {{c1::< 1}} cm in size.
fields.Extra: < 10 mm Usually 1-5 ulcers at a time Take 1-2 weeks to heal, without scarring
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Major aphtous ulcers heal {{c1::with::with/without}} scarring and are {{c1::1-3}} cm in size.
fields.Extra: 1-3 cm (>10mm) Usually 1-10 ulcers at a time Take 2-6 weeks to heal, may scar
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Sutton disease is another name for what oral lesion ? {{c1::Major aphtous ulcers}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Aphtous ulcers on oral and genital mucosa along with ocular inflammation is evocative of what syndrome ? {{c1::Behcet's Syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Behcet's syndrome is a multisystem vasculitis that affects which areas of the body ? - {{c1::Oral mucosa}} - {{c1::Genital mucosa}} - {{c1::Eyes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Erythema multiforme is thought to be a hypersensitivity reaction to infections the most common of which is from {{c1::herpes simplex virus}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How do you differentiante minor and major erythema multiforme ? {{c1::minor only has skin and 1 mucosa site lesion}}
fields.Extra: Minor = skin and most often oral lesions Major = skin and multiple mucosal lesion sites (genitals, eyes, mouth)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What cutaneous lesion is characteristic of erythema multiforme ? {{c1::bulls eye or target}}
fields.Extra: Minor = skin and most often oral lesions Major = skin and multiple mucosal lesion sites (genitals, eyes, mouth)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which blistering, ulcerative mucocutaneous condition is due to a hypersensitivity reaction to infection (eg. HSV) ? {{c1::Erythema multiforme}}
fields.Extra: Minor = skin and most often oral lesions Major = skin and multiple mucosal lesion sites (genitals, eyes, mouth)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis are hypersensitivity reactions to what ? {{c1::drugs}}
fields.Extra: - 200+ identified medications - SJS involves less than 10% of the body surface affected by lesions, and TEN involves more than 30%
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What 2 severe mucocutaneous blistering, ulcerative conditions are due to a hypersensitivity reaction to various drugs/medications ? - {{c1::Stevens-Johnson Syndrome}} - {{c1::Toxic epidermal necrolysis (Lyell Syndrome)}}
fields.Extra: - 200+ identified medications - SJS involves less than 10% of the body surface affected by lesions, and TEN involves more than 30% Erythema multiforme is now considered distinct from Stevens-Johnson and Lyell syndrome
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An allergic reaction due to drugs or food contact that manifests as diffuse swelling of the lips, neck, or face is called {{c1::angioedema}}
fields.Extra: = Angioneurotic edema, Quincke's disease
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Angioedema is due to the release of {{c1::histamines}} and activation of {{c1::IgE}} by/on {{c2::mast-cells}}
fields.Extra: = Angioneurotic edema, Quicke disease = Angioneurotic edema, Quincke's disease
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What rare allergic reaction condition is due to an inhaled antigen and manifests as strawberry gingivitis ? {{c1::Wegener's granulomatosis}}
fields.Extra: "disorder includes necrotizing granulomatous lesions of the respiratory tract , necrotizing glomerulonephritis , and systemic vasculitis of small arteries and veins"
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the characteristic oral manifestation of Wegener's granulomatosis ? {{c1::strawberry gingivitis}}
fields.Extra: "disorder includes necrotizing granulomatous lesions of the respiratory tract, necrotizing glomerulonephritis, and systemic vasculitis of small arteries and veins"
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What immunologic disease is characterized by destruction of basal keratinocytes by T-lymphocytes ? {{c1::Oral lichen planus}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are characteristic cutaneous lesions of oral lichen planus ? {{c1::purple, pruritic, polygonal papules}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What characteristic histological pattern does oral lichen planus present ? {{c1::sawtooth rete pegs}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Reticular oral lichen planus is characterized by interlacing white lines called {{c1::Wickham striae}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Erosive oral lichen planus is characterized by red ulcerations and must be accompanied by what other sign ? {{c1::Wickham striae}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the two main types of lupus erythematosus ? - {{c1::Discoid chronic type}} - {{c1::Systemic acute type}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c3::Discoid chronic}} type Lupus Erythematosus manifests on the facial skin as {{c1::disc-like}} lesions and oral lesions that mimic {{c2::erosive lichen planus}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Systemic acute type Lupus Erythematosus involves multiple organs and in addition to intraoral lesions commonly produces a facial rash in the shape of a {{c1::butterfly}}
fields.Extra: Source: Doktorinternet, CC BY-SA 4.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hardening of skin and connective tissue is termed {{c1::scleroderma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which vesiculo-bullous auto-immune disease is characterized by autoantibodies against desmosomes ? {{c1::Pemphigus vulgaris}}
fields.Extra: Desmosomes = holds keratinocytes together
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which sign is characteristic of pemphigus vulgaris ? {{c1::Nikolsky's sign}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Mucous membrane pemphigoid is an autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibodies directed against the {{c1::basement membrane (hemidesmosomes)}}
fields.Extra: hemidesmosomes = hold keratinocytes to BM
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which vesiculo-bullous auto-immune disease is characterized by autoantibodies against hemidesmosomes (basement membrane) ? {{c1::Mucous membrane pemphigoid}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A white patch that does not rub off of unknown origin is clinically described as {{c1::leukoplakia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A recurrent wharty white plaque that doesn't rub off and is at high risk of malignant transformation is termed {{c1::proliferative verrucous leukoplakia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Proliferative verrucous leukoplakia may be associated with what viral strains ? {{c1::HPV 16, 18}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Proliferative verrucous leukoplakia has a high risk of transformation to what malignancies ? - {{c1::Squamous cell carcinoma}} - {{c1::Verrucous carcinoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A red patch of unknown origin is clinically described as {{c1::erythroplakia}} and is considered premalignant until proven otherwise.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Erythroplakia - Leukoplakia - Erythroleukoplakia Order from greatest to least malignancy risk : 1. {{c1::Erythroleukoplakia}} 2. {{c1::Erythroplakia}} 3. {{c1::Leukoplakia}}
fields.Extra: Potentially malignant --> BIOPSY
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dry, fissured, scaly, white or red lesions on the vermillion of the lips that may be at risk of malignant transformation is called {{c1::actinic cheilitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the cause of actinic cheilitis ? {{c1::UVB radiation (sun) }}
fields.Extra: UV-B -> "B for Bad"
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A keratotic lesion in the buccal vestibule due to a smokeless tobacco habit is termed {{c1::tobacco pouch keratosis}}
fields.Extra: Regresses at cessation (usually within 2 weeks) If lesion remains after 6 weeks, biopsy At risk lesion though " the clinical appearance of smokeless tobacco keratosis is distinct enough and the malignant transformation potential is low enough that biopsy is needed for only severe or atypical lesions (i.e., those demonstrating an intense whiteness, a granular or verruciform clinical appearance, ulceration, mass formation, induration, or hemorrhage)"
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 most at risk oral sites of malignancy ? 1. {{c1::Floor of the mouth}} 2. {{c1::Posterior lateral tongue}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cancers that arise from epithelial tissue are called {{c1::carcinoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cancers that arise from mesenchymal tissue (connective tissue) are called {{c1::sarcomas}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cancers that arise from blood are called {{c1::leukemias}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cancers that arise from the lymphatics are called {{c1::lymphomas}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A pre-cancerous lesion that exhibits cellular atypia or disruption of tissue architecture is said to exhibit {{c1::dysplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When an epithelial cancer affects an entire layer of epithelium it is histologically termed {{c1::carcinoma in situ}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When an epithelial cancer invades past the basement membrane it is histologically termed a {{c1::malignant neoplasm}}
fields.Extra: = invasive carcinoma
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the name of classification for staging cancers ? {{c1::TNM system}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 major causes of Verrucous Carcinoma ? - {{c1::Tobacco}} - {{c1::HPV 16, 18}}
fields.Extra: Can succede proliferative verrucous leukoplakia = snuff dipper's cancer
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is verrucous carcinoma a slow or fast evolving cancer ? {{c1::slow}}
fields.Extra: Can succede proliferative verrucous leukoplakia = snuff dipper's cancer
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Increased incidence of oropharyngeal SCC is associated with which strains of HPV ? {{c1::16 and 18}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Squamous cell carcinoma is caused by {{c1::oncogenes}} or inactivation of {{c1::tumor suppressor}} genes
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which syndrome associates mucosal atrophy , dysphagia , iron deficiency anemia , and increased oral cancer risk ? {{c1::Plummer-Vinson Syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the approximate 5-year survival rate of oral squamous cell carcinoma ? {{c1::50%}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The most common skin cancer results from exposure to UVB radiation is called {{c1::basal cell carcinoma}}
fields.Extra: Slow spreading, 80% on skin of head and neck! Source: John Hendrix, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A malignant neoplasm of melanocytic origins is called a {{c1::melanoma}}
fields.Extra: Acute sun damage may be a greater risk factor than chronic exposure
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 high-risk sites for development of oral melanoma ? - {{c1::Palate}} - {{c1::Gingiva}}
fields.Extra: Acute sun damage may be a greater risk factor than chronic exposure
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is the 5-year survival rate for oral melanoma better or worse than for cutaneous melanoma ? {{c1::Significantly worse (20%)}}
fields.Extra: Acute sun damage may be a greater risk factor than chronic exposure
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A fibroma is caused by {{c1::chronic trauma}} or {{c1::irritation}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A hard pink nodule that arises from chronic trauma or irritation is termed a {{c1::fibroma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A fibroma is due to reactive fibrous {{c1::hyperplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What drugs can lead to gingival hyperplasia ? - {{c1::Anticonvulsants (Phenytoin/Dilantin)}} - {{c1::Calcium channel blockers}} - {{c1::Cyclosporine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for drug-induced gingival hyperplasia ? {{c1::gingivectomy and drug discontinuation if possible}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Denture-induced fibrous hyperplasia of the vestibule is termed {{c1::epulis fissuratum}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Denture-induced fibrous hyperplasia of the palate is termed {{c1::papillary hyperplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An entangled submucosal mass of neural tissue due to nerve injury is called a {{c1::traumatic neuroma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Where is the most common site for a traumatic neuroma ? {{c1::Mental foramen}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Multiple neuromas associated with medullary thyroid cancer and pheochromocytoma is called {{c1::Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN 2B)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN 2B) associates what three conditions ? - {{c1::Multiple neuromas}} - {{c1::Medullary thyroid cancer}} - {{c1::Pheochromocytoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A pyogenic granuloma is due to hyperplasia of {{c1::capillaries}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A bright red, painless, fast growing nodule due to chronic irritation or trauma is called a {{c1::pyogenic granuloma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Nodular fasciitis and fibromatosis are both neoplasms of which cell type ? {{c1::fibroblasts}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which benign neoplasm of fibroblastic origin usually occurs in the superficial fascia and rarely recurs ? {{c1::Nodular Fasciitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which benign neoplasm of fibroblastic origin may exhibit rapid or insidious locally aggressive growth and most frequently affects children and adolescents ? {{c1::Fibromatosis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Granular cell tumors are neoplasms of which cell type ? {{c1::Schwann cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What histological characteristic of granular cell tumors mimics squamous cell carcinoma ? {{c1::Pseudepitheliomatous hyperplasia (PEH)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A neoplasm of Schwann cells that is most common on the dorsal tongue and presents as an asymptomatic sessile nodule is called a {{c1::granular cell tumor}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The variant of {{c2::granular cell tumor}} that is present in newborns on the gingiva is called a {{c1::congenital epulis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign neural neoplasm of Schwann cell origin that contains Verocay bodies in Antoni A tissue is called a {{c1::Schwannoma (Neurilemoma)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Schwannomas (Neurilemomas) contain acellular {{c1::Verocay}} bodies in {{c2::Antoni A}} tissue.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A neurofibroma is a neoplasm of what type of cells ? {{c1::Schwann cell AND fibroblasts}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Neurofibromatosis type I is also called {{c1::Von Recklinghausen's}} disease
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which disease associates multiple neurofibromas , café au lait spots , axillary freckles (Crowe's sign) and iris freckles (lisch spots) ? {{c1::Neurofibromatosis type I/Von Recklinhausen's disease}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign neoplasm of smooth muscle cells is termed a {{c1::leiomyoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign neoplasm of skeletal muscle cells is termed a {{c1::rhabdomyoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign neoplasm of fat cells is termed a {{c1::lipoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A malignant proliferation of fibroblasts is called a {{c1::fibrosarcoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A malignant proliferation of Scwhann cells is called a {{c1::neurofibrosarcoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The purple malignant proliferation of endothelial cells caused by HHV8 is called {{c1::Kaposi sarcoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A leiomyosarcoma is a malignant proliferation of what cells ? {{c1::smooth muscle cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A rhabdomyosarcoma is a malignant proliferation of what cells ? {{c1::skeletal muscle cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A liposarcoma is a malignant proliferation of what cells ? {{c1::fat cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A fibrosarcoma is a malignant proliferation of what cells ? {{c1::fibroblasts}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A neurofibrosarcoma is a malignant proliferation of what cells ? {{c1::Scwhann cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A nodule formed by trauma to a salivary duct that is not lined by an epithelium is called {{c1::mucous extravasation phenomenon}}
fields.Extra: Source: Dozenist, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What causes mucous extravasation phenomenon ? {{c1::trauma to salivary ducts}}
fields.Extra: Source: Dozenist, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A mucous extravasation phenomenon is also commonly called a {{c1::mucocele}}
fields.Extra: Source: Dozenist, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A mucous extravasation phenomenon that occurs on the floor of the mouth is termed a {{c1::ranula}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A nodule formed after blockage of a salivary duct called {{c1::mucous retention cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What causes a mucous retention cyst ? {{c1::blockage of a salivary duct by a sialolith}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A rapidly expanding ulcerative lesion due to ischemic necrosis of minor salivary glands is called {{c1::necrotizing sialometaplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What may cause necrotizing sialometaplasia ? {{c1::trauma or anesthesia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for necrotizing sialometaplasia ? {{c1::Symptomatic, heals within 6-10 weeks}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Necrotizing sialometaplasia is due to ischemic necrosis of what elements ? {{c1::minor salivary glands}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What sinus radiopacity is due to subepithelial accumulation of inflammatory exudate ( serum ) ? {{c1::Antral pseudocyst}}
fields.Extra: Not a true cyst
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An antral pseudocyst is due to the subepithelial accumulation of {{c1::serum (inflammatory exudate)}}
fields.Extra: Not a true cyst
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which sinus radiopacity arises from an obstruction of the sinus ostium resulting in accumulation of mucus ? {{c1::sinus mucocele}}
fields.Extra: Enlarge in size as pressure increases and can disten the walls of the sinus, even eroding through bone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Accumulation of mucus in sinus mucoceles are due to what ? {{c1::obstruction of the sinus ostium}}
fields.Extra: - Arises from an obstruction of the sinus ostium resulting in accumulation of mucus - Enlarge in size as pressure increases and can disten the walls of the sinus, even eroding through bone (Neville and Damm)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A sinus retention cyst is due to partial blockage of a {{c1::seromucous gland}} duct within the sinus wall or from an invagination of respiratory epithelium.
fields.Extra: - Most are located around the ostium or within antral polyps - Most are small, not evident clinically NOT THE SAME AS ANRTAL PSEUDOCYST
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A sinus lesion due to partial blockage of a seromucous gland duct within the sinus wall or from an invagination of respiratory epithelium is called a {{c1::sinus retention cyst}}
fields.Extra: - Most are located around the ostium or within antral polyps - Most are small, not evident clinically NOT THE SAME AS ANTRAL PSEUDOCYST
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which multisystem granulomatous disorder that mainly affects the lungs is thought to be due to to a hyperimmune state ? {{c1::Sarcoidosis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What oral symptom often accompanies sarcoidosis ? {{c1::Xerostomia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What sarcoidosis associated syndrome associates erythema nodosum , bilateral hilar lymphoadenopathy , and arthritis ? {{c1::Lofgren's Syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What sarcoidosis associated syndrome associates uveitis, parotid gland enlargement, facial nerve palsy, and fever ? {{c1::Heerfordt's syndorme}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Sjogren's syndrome is an autoimmune condition mediated by which cells ? {{c1::lymphocytes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which glands does Sjogren's syndrome affect ? {{c1::salivary}} and {{c1::tear glands}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which autoimmune condition causes keratoconjonctivitis sicca and xerostomia ? {{c1:: Primary Sjogren's Syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When Sjogren's syndrome is associated with another autoimmune disease such as rheumatoid arthritis it is referred to as {{c1::secondary sjogren's syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the two major symptoms of Sjogren's Syndrome ? - {{c1::Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes)}} - {{c1::Xerostomia (dry mouth)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common benign salivary gland tumor ? {{c1::Pleomorphic adenoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What tissue types make up pleomorphic adenoma ? {{c1::connective and epithelial tissue}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The consistency of pleomorphic adenoma is described as {{c1::firm}} and {{c1::rubbery}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The most common site for minor salivary gland pleomorphic adenomas is where ? {{c1::palate}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The most common site for parotid gland pleomorphic adenomas swelling is where ? {{c1::around the ear}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which single-cell type benign salivary gland tumor regroups many different tumors ? {{c1::Monomorphic adenoma}}
fields.Extra: Ambigous name, should be avoided
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Warthin's tumors are composed of what cell types ? - {{c1::Oncocytes}} - {{c1::Lymphoid cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Warthin's tumor almost exclusively affects which salivary gland ? {{c1::parotid}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An oncocyte is an epithelial cell with excessive number of {{c1::mitochondria}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Malignant salivary gland tumors most often occur on which oral site ? {{c1::Palate}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common salivary gland malignancy ? {{c1::mucoepidermoid carcinoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What cells are responsible for mucoepidermoid carcinomas ? {{c1::mucous and epithelial cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the second most common malignant salivary gland tumor ? {{c1::Polymorhpous low-grade adenocarcinoma (PLGA)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An adenoid cystic carcinoma is characterized by what kind of microsopic pattern ? {{c1::cribriform or swiss cheese}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Most lymphoid neoplasms occur in the {{c1::lymph nodes}} but occasionally they arise in extranodal tissue called {{c2::MALTs (mucosa associated lymphoid tissue)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hodgkin's lymphoma is due to what type of malignant cells ? {{c1::B cells}}
fields.Extra: Source: National Cancer Institute, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What neoplasm is due to malignant B cells called Reed-Sternberg cells ? {{c1::Hogdkin's Lymphoma}}
fields.Extra: Source: National Cancer Institute, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the specific name for the malignant B cells in Hodgkin's lymphoma ? {{c1::Reed-Sternberg cells}}
fields.Extra: Source: National Cancer Institute, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Where are the most common sites of initial presentation of Hodgkin's Lymphoma ? {{c1::cervical and supraclavicular lymph nodes (70-75%)}}
fields.Extra: Source: National Cancer Institute, Public Domain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma is a neoplasm of what cells ? {{c1::B or T cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which category of neoplasms affects either B or T cells that usually begins in lymph nodes and tend to grow as solid masses ? {{c1::Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma is due to B cells and represents an undifferentiated lymphoma (involves the bone marrow) ? {{c1::Burkitt's Lymphoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Burkitt's lymphoma is due to what type of malignant cell ? {{c1::B cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Burkitt's lymphoma is associated with what dental signs ? - {{c1::Halted root development}} - {{c1::Tooth mobility}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of neoplasm involves antibody-secreting B cells (plasma cells) ? {{c1::Multiple Myeloma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Multiple myeloma is due to a malignancy of what cells ? {{c1::plasma cells (antibody secreting B cells)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What characteristic radiographic feature appears in patients with multiple myeloma ? {{c1::Punched out radiolucencies}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common presenting symptom for patients with multiple myeloma ? {{c1::Bone pain (especially lumbar spine)}}
fields.Extra: May be painful in older adults
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Renal failure in multiple myeloma patients is common due to the excess of circulating light-chain proteins called {{c1::Bence Jones proteins}}, present in 30-50% of patient's urine.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In multiple myeloma patients, what process is responsible for the formation of nodules , especially on the tongue ? {{c1::Amyloidosis}}
fields.Extra: May be painful in older adults
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Leukemia is a neoplasm of what broad category of cells ? {{c1::bone marrow cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What category of neoplasms is due to malignant bone marrow cells ? {{c1::Leukemias}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The classification of leukemias is based on what criteria ? - {{c1::Cell lineage (myeloid/lymphoid)}} - {{c1::Acute/chronic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Classify which leukemias affect patients based on age. Young {{c1::ALL > CML > AML > CLL}} Old
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the three major clinical signs of leukemia ? - {{c1::Bleeding}} - {{c1::Fatigue}} - {{c1::Infection}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cysts by definition are cavities lined by an {{c1::epithelium}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A radicular cyst is also called a {{c1::periapical cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the pulpal status of a tooth exhibiting a radicular/periapical cyst ? {{c1::Nonvital}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Where does the epithelium that lines a radicular/periapical cyst come from ? {{c1::Epithelial Rests of Malassez}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A radicular/periapical cyst is radiographically indistinguishable to what other periapical lesion associated with a non-vital tooth ? {{c1::periapical granuloma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common odontogenic cyst ? {{c1::radicular/periapical cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A dentigerous cyst is attached to or centered on what part of an unerupted tooth ? {{c1::cemento-enamel junction (CEJ)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A dentigerous cyst is due to fluid accumulation between the crown and which epithelium ? {{c1::Reduced enamel epithelium}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A dentigerous cyst is also commonly called a {{c1::follicular cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dentigerous cysts are most common in which teeth ? - {{c1::Third molars}} - {{c1::Canines}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for dentigerous cysts ? {{c1::Excision}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for periapical/radicular cysts ? {{c1::RCT, apicoectomy, extraction with curettage}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The soft-tissue equivalent to a dentigerous cyst is called an {{c1::eruption cyst}} and occurs mostly in children under 10.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What odontogenic cyst presents as soft, often translucent swelling in the gingival mucosa overlying the crown of an unerupted tooth ? {{c1::Eruption cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An odontogenic cyst most common in the mandibular premolar associated with a vital tooth is often a {{c1::lateral periodontal cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the pulpal status of a tooth exhibiting a lateral periodontal cyst ? {{c1::Vital}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Lateral periodontal cysts are most common in which region of the mouth ? {{c1::Mandibular premolars}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The soft tissue counterpart of a lateral periodontal cyst is called a {{c1::gingival cyst of the adult}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Lateral periodontal cysts and gingival cyst of the adult and gingival cyst of newborn are both odontogenic cysts that are thought to arise from what tissue ? {{c1::Dental lamina}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gingival cyst of the Newborn arise from remnants of what tissue ? {{c1::Dental lamina}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gingival cyst of the Newborn that arise on the lateral palate are termed {{c1::Bohn's nodules}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gingival cyst of the Newborn that arise on the midline palate are termed {{c1::Epstein's pearls}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment is recommended for Gingival cyst of the newborn ? {{c1::None, will involute as the infant ages}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An odontogenic cyst that develops where a tooth would have formed (but didn't) is called a {{c1::primordial cyst}}
fields.Extra: "Almost all examples of so-called primordial cysts (i.e., a cyst that develops in the place of a tooth) microscopically will be OKCs." --> 2017 classification does not include them
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Primordial cysts are most common in what region ? {{c1::mandibular 3rd molar region}}
fields.Extra: "Almost all examples of so-called primordial cysts (i.e., a cyst that develops in the place of a tooth) microscopically will be OKCs." --> 2017 classification does not include them
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for primordial cysts ? {{c1::Complete removal}}
fields.Extra: "Almost all examples of so-called primordial cysts (i.e., a cyst that develops in the place of a tooth) microscopically will be OKCs." --> 2017 classification does not include them
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What odontogenic cyst is aggressive and recurrent and is most common in the posterior ascending ramus of the mandible ? {{c1::Odontogenic keratocyst}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Odontogenic keratocysts are most common in what area ? {{c1::Posterior ascending ramus of the mandible}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Odontogenic keratocysts arise from what type of tissue ? {{c1::Dental lamina}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Histologically, odontogenic keratocysts are chacterized by a {{c1::corrugated}} {{c2::parakeratinized}} epithelium
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for odontogenic keratocysts ? {{c1::Aggressive enucleation (high recurrence)}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which syndrome associates multiple OKCs , multiple basal cell carcinomas, and calcified falx cerebri ? {{c1::Gorlin's syndrome (nevoid basal cell carcinoma)}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gorlin's syndrome or nevoid basal cell carcinoma is characterized by multiple {{c2:: odontogenic keratocysts }}, multiple {{c3:: basal cell carcinomas }} , and {{c1:: calcified falx cerebri }}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gorlin's syndrome is also called {{c1::nevoid basal cell carcinoma}}
fields.Extra: Odontogenic keratocyst in the 2017 classification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which odontogenic cyst is characterized by ghost cells whose nuclei fill with keratin and can undergo calcification ? {{c1::calcifying odontogenic cyst}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c3::Calcifying odontogenic cysts}} are characterized by {{c2:: ghost }} cells whose nuclei fill with {{c1:: keratin }} and can undergo calcification
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Are ameloblastomas benign or malignant ? {{c1::benign but aggressive }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for ameloblastomas ? {{c1::wide excision or resection, or else high recurrence}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common odontogenic tumor (not counting odontomas) ? {{c1::Ameloblastoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Radiographically, ameloblastomas are described as demonstrating what pattern ? {{c1::soap bubble (large) or honeycomb (small)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the classic differential diagnosis for multilocular radiolucencies of the posterior mandible ? - {{c1::Ameloblastoma}} - {{c1::Odontogenic keratocyst (OKC)}} - {{c1::Central giant cell granuloma (CGCG)}} - {{c1::Central odontogenic fibroma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Ameloblastomas are of mesenchymal or epithelial origin ? {{c1::Epithelial}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (CEOT) are also called {{c1::Pindborg tumors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c2::Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (CEOT)}} presents as a radiolucency with calcifications described as {{c1:: driven snow }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Histologically, {{c3::calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (CEOT)}} show epithelial cells in an {{c1:: amorphous pink amyloid }} extracellular material with concentric calcifications termed {{c2:: Liesegang rings }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Adenomatoid odontogenic tumors occur most frequently in the {{c1::anterior maxilla}} and over impacted {{c1::canines}}.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c3::Adenomatoid odontogenic tumors}} contain characteristic histological features that appear {{c1:: tubular or duct-like::shape }} as well as calcifications that are likely {{c2:: enameloid }} material.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Odontogenic myxomas are also called {{c1::myxofibromas}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c3::Odontogenic myxomas}} are composed of {{c1::pulp}}-like myxomatous connective tissue that is described as a {{c2:: slimy stroma }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Radiographically, odontogenic myxomas have {{c1:: unclear ::clear/unclear}} borders and a {{c2:: honeycomb }} pattern
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Radiographically, central odontogenic fibromas (COF) are described as a {{c1::well::well/poorly}} defined {{c2::multilocular::uni/multi-locular}} radiolucencies
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Histologically, central odontogenic fibromas are composed of dense {{c1::collagen}} with cords of epithelium.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign neoplasm of cementoblasts is called a {{c1::cementoblastoma}}
fields.Extra: = true cementoma
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A well circumscribed radiopaque mass that is fused to and replaces a tooth root is termed a {{c1::cementoblastoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for cementoblastoma ? {{c1::Excision and extraction of the tooth}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Most cementoblastomas involve the mandible and over 50% affect which tooth ? {{c1::mandibular first molar}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Ameloblastic fibroma most often occurs in what age group ? {{c1::children and teens}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Ameloblastic fibroma are most common in which part of the mouth ? {{c1::Posterior mandible}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Histologically, ameloblastic fibromas are composed of {{c1::myxomatous}} connective tissue
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When an ameloblastic fibroma is associated with an odontoma is is called an {{c1::ameloblastic fibro-odontoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which odontogenic tumors are composed mainly of enamel and dentin with variable amounts of pulp and cementum ? {{c1::Odontomas}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of odontoma radiographically appears as a bunch of miniature teeth ? {{c1::compound odontoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of odontoma radiographically appears as a conglomerate mass ? {{c1::complex odontoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Compound odontomas are most often found {{c1::anteriorly::anteriorly or posteriorly ?}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Complex odontomas are most often found {{c1::posteriorly::anteriorly or posteriorly ?}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which syndrome associates multiple odontomas and intestinal polyps ? {{c1::Gardner syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Gardner syndrome manifests as multiple {{c1::odontomas}} and {{c2::intestinal polyps}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Fibro-osseous bone lesions are benign tumors composed of fibrous tissue in which {{c1::bone}} develops.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Central ossifying fibroma are composed of a {{c1::fibroblastic::cell type}} stroma in which foci of mineralized products are formed
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The soft-tissue variant of central ossifying fibroma is termed {{c1::peripheral ossifying fibroma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which form of central ossifying fibroma is often aggressive and exhibits rapid growth ? {{c1::Juvenile central ossifying fibroma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The radiographic appearance of fibrous dysplasia is described as {{c1::ground-glass}} appearance.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Fibrous dysplasia lesions usually stop growing after {{c1::puberty (skeletal maturation)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for fibrous dysplasia ? {{c1::surgical recontouring for cosmetics}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What syndrome associates polyostotic fibrous dysplasia , endocrine abnormalities , and cutaneous café au lait spots ? {{c1::McCune-Albright Syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: McCune-Albright syndrome associates what characteristics ? - {{c1::Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia}} - {{c2::Cutaneous café au lait spot}} - {{c3::Endocrine abnormalities}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Periapical cemento-osseous dysplasia (PCOD) is most common at the apices of which teeth ? {{c1::Mandibular anteriors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Periapical cemento-osseous dysplasia (PCOD) is most common in which ethnic and age group ? {{c1::Middle-aged black females}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the pulpal diagnosis of Periapical Cemento-Osseous Dysplasia (PCOD) ? {{c1::Vital}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for periapical cemento-osseous dysplasia (PCOD) ? {{c1::None}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: As periapical cemento-osseous dysplasia (PCOD) evolves, the radiographic lesion goes from {{c1::radiolucent::radiolucent/radiopaque}} to {{c1::radiopaque::radiolucent/radiopaque}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A benign circumscribed opaque mass of bone and osteoblasts is termed an {{c1::osteoblastoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Treatment for osteoblastomas ? {{c1::Excision}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Central giant cell granulomas are composed of what cells ? {{c1::fibroblasts}} and {{c1::multinucleated giant cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Central giant cell granulomas are most common in the anterior or posterior mandible ? {{c1::Anterior mandible}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which giant cell lesion manifests as a radiolucency with thin, wispy septations most common in the anterior mandible ? {{c1::Central giant cell granuloma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An aneurysmal bone cyst is filled with what ? {{c1::Blood}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Radiographically, an aneurysmal bone cyst presents as a {{c1::multilocular::uni/multi-locular}} expansile radiolucency
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Aneurysmal bone cysts are most common in the anterior or posterior mandible ? {{c1::Posterior mandible}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What endocrine disorder causes multiple bone lesions and radiographically is similar to CGCG ? {{c1::Hyperparathyroidism}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Brown tumors are due to excess activity of which cell type ? {{c1::osteoclasts}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Excess activity of osteoclasts as seen in hyperparathyroidism leads to the formation of radiolucencies that are termed {{c1::brown}} tumors
fields.Extra: Called brown tumors because of the color of the tissue upon excision (due to abundant hemorrhage and hemosiderin deposition within the tumor)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hyperparathyroidism leads to elevated levels of which enzyme due to the excessive bone breakdown ? {{c1::Alkaline phosphatase}}
fields.Extra: Excess osteoclastic activity due to excess paratyhroid function
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which pathology is associated with symmetric, bilateral, expansile bilateral radiolucencies with swelling ? {{c1::Cherubism}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What cells are at the origin of cherubism ? {{c1::giant cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When do the lesions from cherubism stop growing ? {{c1::puberty}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Langherans cell disease is also called {{c1::idiopathic histiocytosis}} and is a rare type of cancer .
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The radiolucencies described in Langherans cell disease are described as punched out {{c1::ice cream scoop}} shaped radiolucencies
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The "ice cream scoop" radiolucencies as found in langherans cell disease leads to what dental radiographic sign ? {{c1::floating teeth}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatent for Langherans cell disease ? {{c1::Excision, radiation, chemotherapy}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which pathology is a progressive metabolic disturbance of many bones causing symmetric enlargement ? {{c1::Paget's disease}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In Paget's disease , what enzyme is released in excess as bone breaks down ? {{c1::Alkaline phosphatase}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Paget's disease is radiographically described as having a {{c1::cotton wool}} appearance
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient complaining of dentures and/or hats becoming too tight is evocative of what bone pathology ? {{c1::Paget's disease}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What combination treatment for Paget's disease ? {{c1::Bisphosphonates and Calcitonin (Vit D)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The most common cause of acute suppurative osteomyelitis is {{c1::odontogenic infection}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Deep, intense pain, associated with systemic symptoms caused by infection of bone and dating less than 4 weeks is termed {{c1::acute suppurative osteomyelitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which clinical sign describes unilateral anesthesia of the inferor lip ? {{c1::Vincent's sign}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the three cardinal signs of systemic involvement of an infection ? {{c1:: F ever}} {{c1:: M alaise}} {{c1:: L ymphoadenopathy}}
fields.Extra: Indication for empiric antibiotics
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for acute suppurative osteomyelitis ? {{c1::antibiotics and drainage}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What arbitrary amount of time separates acute suppurative osteomyelitis and chronic suppurative osteomyelitis ? {{c1::4 weeks}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When osteomyelitis compresses the inferior alveolar nerve , what clinical sign may be present ? {{c1::Vincent's sign}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A diffuse mottled radiolucency due to bone infection is due to what pathology ? {{c1::chronic suppurative osteomyelitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Fragments of necrotic bone that have separated from the adjacent vital bone as seen in chronic suppurative osteomyelitis are called {{c1::sequestra}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Chronic osteomyelitis with {{c2::proliferative periostitis}} is called {{c1:: Garre's osteomyelitis }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for chronic suppurative osteomyelitis ? {{c1::antibiotics and debridement}}
fields.Extra: Antibiotics alone are insufficient as there is no distribution in the necrotic parts of the lesion
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Condensing osteitis is also called {{c1::focal sclerosing osteomyelitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A periapical radiopaque lesion representing a localized bony reaction to a low-grade inflammatory stimulus is called {{c1::condensing osteitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for condensing osteitis ? {{c1::None, address the cause}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A more diffuse variety of condensing osteitis is termed {{c1::diffuse sclerosing osteomyelitis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Necrosis of bone due to bisphosphonates is termed {{c1::Bisphosphonate-related osteoncresis of the jaws (BRONJ)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the suffix denoting bisphophonate drugs ? {{c1::-dronate}}
fields.Extra: eg. Residronate, Alendronate
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is the risk of developing MRONJ greater with oral or IV administration ? {{c1::IV, usually administered for treating malignancies}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for BRONJ ? {{c1::CHX rinse, antibiotics, conservative surgery}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most frequent presenting symptom of malignant lesions of the mandible ? {{c1::Lip paresthesia/anesthesia}}
fields.Extra: Vincent's sign
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which bone malignancy results in new bone formation (osteoid or immature bone) by tumor cells ? {{c1::Osteosarcoma}}
fields.Extra: = osteogenic sarcoma
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What radiographic pattern is often associated with osteosarcomas ? {{c1::sunburst pattern}}
fields.Extra: = osteogenic sarcoma
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which malignancy of the jaws is associated with new cartilage production by tumor cells ? {{c1::Chondrosarcoma}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Chondrosarcomas are more common in which orofacial structure ? {{c1::Condyles}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Ewing's sarcoma is a malignancy that affects which types of bones ? {{c1::long bones (rarely affects the jaws)}}
fields.Extra: Source: Michael Richardson, M.D., CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which malignancy of long bones mostly affects children and involves swelling ? {{c1::Ewing's sarcoma}}
fields.Extra: Source: Michael Richardson, M.D., CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c2::Ewing's sarcoma}} is a malignancy of long bones involving small, undifferentiated cells called {{c1::round cells}}
fields.Extra: Source: Michael Richardson, M.D., CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Metastatic carcinoma is most common from what primary malignancies ? 1. {{c1::Breast}} 2. {{c1::Lung}} 3. {{c1::Kidney}} 4. {{c1::Colon}} 5. {{c1::Prostate}}
fields.Extra: BLK-CP --> B u LK C om P osites
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Metastatic carcinoma of the mandible is associated with pain , swelling , and what neural sign ? {{c1::Paresthesia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A hereditary , asymptomatic spongy, white, lesion of the buccal mucosa that does not wipe off is called {{c1::white sponge nevus}}
fields.Extra: Due to mutations of keratin 4 and/or 13
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What group of mucocutaneous blistering conditions is hereditary ? {{c1::Epidermolysis bullosa}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia is also called {{c1::Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A red macule or papule due to dilated capillaries are called {{c1::telangiectasias}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which hereditary condition causes abnormal capillary formation on skin, mucosa, and viscera ? {{c1::Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia is associated with what type of anemia ? {{c1::Iron-deficiency anema}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What extraoral sign often accompanies hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia ? {{c1::Epistaxis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which hereditary condition is associated with missing clavicles and supernumerary teeth ? {{c1::Cleidocranial dysplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with cleidocranial dysplasia often present with missing {{c1::clavicles}} and {{c1::supernumerary}} teeth
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which hereditary condition is associated with hypodontia and conical crowns and hypoplastic hair or nails ? {{c1::Ectodermal dysplasia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with ectodermal dysplasia present with {{c1::missing}} teeth and {{c1::hypoplastic}} hair or nails
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Albergs-Schonberg disease and marble bone disease are synonyms for what condition ? {{c1::Osteopetrosis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Osteopetrosis is due to the lack of what process ? {{c1::Bone remodeling and resorption}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which teeth are affected by amelogenesis imperfecta ? {{c1::All teeth from both dentitions}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which dental organs are affected by amelogenesis imperfecta ? {{c1::Only enamel. (Dentin and pulp are normal)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Treatment of amelogenesis imperfecta is often {{c1::full coverage crowns}} for cosmetics.
fields.Extra: Bonding to the altered enamel is complicated and unpredictable
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dentinogenesis imperfecta affects which dental organs ? {{c1::Dentin, which leads to obliterated pulps}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Radiographically, dentinogenesis imperfecta leads to {{c1::shorter::lengthened/shorter}} roots, {{c2::bell}}-shaped crowns, and {{c3::obliterated}} pulps
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What ocular sign is associated with both osteogenesis imperfecta and dentinogenesis imperfecta ? {{c1::Blue sclera}}
fields.Extra: Blue sclera in patient with osteogenesis imperfecta Image source : Liu CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dentin dysplasia is associated with {{c1::chevron}} shaped pulps and {{c2::short::short/long}} roots.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dentin dysplasia affects which teeth ? {{c1::all teeth from both dentitions}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Regional odontodysplasia affects which teeth ? {{c1::a quadrant (several contiguous teeth)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with {{c3::regional odontodysplasia}} have teeth that exhibit {{c1::short::short/long}} roots, open apices , and {{c2::enlarged::smaller/enlarged}} pulp chambers
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The teeth of patients with regional odontodysplasia are nicknamed {{c1::ghost teeth}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for regional odontodysplasia ? {{c1::extraction of affected teeth}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Two tooth buds merging into one tooth is called {{c1::fusion}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: One root bud that has two crowns is called {{c1::gemination}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What developmental alteration is represented ? {{c1::Fusion}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What developmental alteration is represented ? {{c1::Gemination}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is regional odontodysplasia hereditary ? {{c1::No}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which inverse agonist of opioids is used to reverse an overdose ? {{c1::Naloxone}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Before onset, what sensation of nitrous oxide is felt ? {{c1::tingling}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common side effect of nitrous oxide ? {{c1::Nausea}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Long term exposure of nitrous oxide can lead to {{c1::peripheral neuropathy}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common concentration of nitrous oxide administered to children ? {{c1::30%}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common concentration of nitrous oxide administered to adults ? {{c1::50%}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 3-5 minutes of 100% O2 is administered after nitrous oxide therapy to avoid {{c1::diffusion hypoxia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Pharmacokinetics refers to what the {{c1::body}} does to the {{c1::drug}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How a drug is delivered is termed {{c1::administration}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In order to be absorbed into the body, drugs must cross {{c1::epithelial}} and/or {{c1::endothelial}} layers to enter the bloodstream.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the three methods of movement of a drug through a plasma membrane ? - {{c1::Passive diffusion}} - {{c1::Facilitated diffusion}} - {{c1::Active transport}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In order to cross a plasma membrane by passive diffusion a drug must be {{c1::neutrally}} charged.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Most drugs pass through plasma membrane through which process ? {{c1::passive diffusion}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The fraction of a drug dose that reaches the {{c2::general circulation}} divided by total amount administered is termed the {{c1:: bioavailability }}.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which mode of administration has 100% bioavailability ? {{c1::intravenous}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An acidic drug in an acidic environment will be in its ionized or non-ionized form ? {{c1::non-ionized}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A basic drug in a basic environment will be in its ionized or non-ionized form ? {{c1::non-ionized}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: For optimal absorption, the pKa of a weak acid drug should be {{c1::greater than::less than or greater than}} the pH ?
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: For optimal absorption, the pKa of a weak base drug should be {{c1::less than::less than or greater than}} the pH ?
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The way a drug gets to its target site is termed {{c1::distribution}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In orally administered drugs, which process is responsible for decreasing the amount of drug before it reaches the systemic circulation ? {{c1::First pass effect}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Drugs undergo the first pass effect are absorbed in the digestive system and brought to the liver through the {{c1::hepatic portal system}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which mode of drug administration will be undergo the first pass effect ? {{c1::oral}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the three water compartments in the human body ? - {{c1::Plasma (4%)}} - {{c1::Interstitial (16%)}} - {{c1::Intracellular (40%)}}
fields.Extra: water = 60% total mass
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What proportion of body weight is water for an average 75kg man ? {{c1::60%}}
