fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The theoretical volume that would be necessary to contain the total amount of an administered drug at the same concentration that it is observed in the blood plasma is called the {{c1::volume of distribution (V d )}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What three broad categories of people generally have less body water than the "average man" ? - {{c1::Women}} - {{c1::Older people}} - {{c1::Obese people}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which tissue type has the lowest water content ? {{c1::adipose tissue (fat)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which tissue types have the highest water content ? {{c1::brain and muscle}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A high volume of distribution means that a drug is highly tissue-bound or that it remains mostly in the circulation ? {{c1::highly-tissue bound}}
fields.Extra: Binds to tissues and so blood concentration is low, so the theoretical volume needed to get to the same concentration as plasma will be high (high V d )
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A low volume of distribution means that a drug is highly tissue-bound or that it remains mostly in the circulation ? {{c1::remains mostly in the circulation}}
fields.Extra: Stays in the circulation so the volume needed to dilute to the same concentration as plasma is low
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is a drug bound to serum proteins available for distribution to extravascular tissue sites ? {{c1::No}}
fields.Extra: - Acidic drugs bind to albumin - Basic drugs bind to alpha-1 acid glycoprotein, lipoproteins
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How does increasing binding to serum proteins affect the volume of distribution ? {{c1::Lowers the Vd}}
fields.Extra: - Acidic drugs bind to albumin - Basic drugs bind to alpha-1 acid glycoprotein, lipoproteins
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The process by which a drug molecule is chemically altered by the body is termed {{c1::metabolism}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does drug metabolism decrease or increase polarity of the drug ? {{c1::Increases}}
fields.Extra: Leads to less passive diffusion which means more gets excreted
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The process by which a drug undergoes oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis reactions are grouped under the term {{c1::functionalization (Phase 1)}} reactions
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What three types of reactions occur during functionalization / Phase 1 of drug metabolism ? - {{c1::Reduction}} - {{c1::Oxidation}} - {{c1::Hydrolysis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What enzyme superfamily is responsible for oxidation reactions during phase 1 of drug metabolism ? {{c1::Cytochrome P450}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Phase 2 metabolism reactions are called {{c1::conjugations}} because they involve adding hydrophilic molecules to the drug.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common molecule added to drugs during phase 2 conjugation metabolism ? {{c1::glucuronic acid}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which enzyme is responsible for adding glucuronic acid to drugs during phase 2 metabolism reactions ? {{c1::UDP-glucouronosyltransferase}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Phase 1 metabolism facilitates elimination through which waste product ? {{c1::urine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Phase 2 metabolism facilitates elimination through which waste product ? {{c1::feces}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c1:: First }}-order kinetics involve a constant {{c2:: fraction }} of drug being eliminated per unit time
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c1:: Zero }}-order kinetics involve a constant {{c2:: amount }} of drug being eliminated per unit time
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The risk of drug accumulation is greater in drugs that follow {{c1::zero}}-order kinetics
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Drug-drug interactions most commonly affect which step of the pharmacokinetics ? {{c1::Metabolism}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When a drug increases the activity of cytochromes resulting in increased metabolism and reduced effect of another drug, this process is called {{c1::induction}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Induction of cytochromes by another drug has what effect on a drug ? {{c1::reduced effect}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Inhibition of cytochromes by another drug has what effect on a drug ? {{c1::increased toxicity}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When a drug competes for metabolism or directly blocks cytochromes leading to increased toxicity of another drug, this process is called {{c1::inhibition}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Pharmacodynamics refers to what the {{c1::drug}} does to the {{c1::body}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Almost all drug targets are what kind of molecules ? {{c1::proteins}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of drug mimics 100% of the effect of an endogenous agonist molecule ? {{c1::full agonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug that mimics <100% of the effect of an endogenous agonist molecule ? {{c1::partial agonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug that inhibits normal function of an endogenous agonist is called an {{c1::antagonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of antagonist works by decreasing access of the endogenous agonist by binding at the same site ? {{c1::Competitive antagonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of antagonist works by binding to a separate site that prevents binding of the agonist ? {{c1::Non-competitive antagonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The y-axis of a type I dose response curve indicates the {{c1::response/efficacy of the drug}}.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What shape does a type I dose response curve have ? {{c1::sigmoid curve (in log form)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which term denotes the relative ability of a drug-receptor complex to produce a maximum functional response by an endogenous agonist ? {{c1::Intrinsic activity (Emax)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug with an intrinsic activity of 1 is termed a {{c1::full agonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug with an intrinsic activity between 0 and 1 is termed a {{c1::partial agonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug with an intrinsic activity less than 0 is termed a {{c1::inverse agonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A drug with an intrinsic activity of exactly 0 is termed a {{c1::neutral antagonist}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The amount of effect a drug has as a function of binding to its receptor is termed {{c1::efficacy}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The attractiveness of a drug to its receptor is termed {{c1::affinity}} and is described by the dissociation constant (Kd)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The {{c1::lower}} the dissociation constant (Kd) , the higher the affinity.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The power of a drug at a specific concentration is called the {{c1::potency}} which is measured by the effective concentration .
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The more potent a drug, the {{c1::lower}} its EC50 value.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a type I dose response curve, a competitive antagonist shifts the agonist curve in which direction ? {{c1::to the right}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a type I dose response curve, a non-competitive antagonist shifts the agonist curve in which direction ? {{c1::down}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The y-axis of a type II dose response curve indicates what ? {{c1::number of subjects responding to that dose}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What three doses are plotted on a type II dose response curve ? - {{c1::Effective dose (EC50)}} - {{c1::Toxic dose (TD50)}} - {{c1::Lethal dose (LD50)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which dose response curve measures efficacy over dose ? {{c1::Type I}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which dose response curve measures proportion of patients responding over dose ? {{c1::Type II}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In animal studies the therapeutic index is defined as the {{c1::LD50}} divided by the {{c1::ED50}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In human studies the therapeutic index is defined as the {{c1::TD50}} divided by the {{c1::ED50}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The range of drug dosages which optimizes efficacy and toxicity is termed the {{c1::therapeutic window}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Two drugs that are {{c1::additive}} means that combining those drugs combines their individual degrees of effect
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Two drugs that are {{c1::antagonistic}} means that combining those drugs causes a lesser effect than either one alone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Two drugs that are {{c1::synergistic}} means that combining those drugs causes an effect greater than the sum of both independently
fields.Extra: eg. APAP + Ibuprofen are more effective in reducing 3rd molar post-op pain than opioids
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which nervous system is responsible for the "fight or flight" response ? {{c1::Sympathetic nervous system}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which nervous system is responsible for the "feed and breed" or "rest and digest" response ? {{c1::Parasympathetic nervous system}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The vasculature is controlled exclusively by which nervous system ? {{c1::Sympathetic nervous system}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which structures are induced by both the SNS and the PSNS ? {{c1::Sweat glands}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The parasympathetic nervous system originates from which brain/spinal nerves ? {{c1::cranial and sacral nerves}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The sympathetic nervous system originates from which spinal nerves ? {{c1::lumbar and thoracic nerves}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which SNS or PSNS nerves originate from the cervical branches ? {{c1::None!}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Ionotropic receptors belong to what category of receptor ? {{c1::ion channels}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Metabotropic receptors belong to what category of receptor ? {{c1::G-protein coupled receptor}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the two types of cholinergic receptors in the autonomic nervous system ? - {{c1::Nicotinic}} - {{c1::Muscarinic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Nicotinic receptors are ionotropic or metabotropic ? {{c1::Ionotropic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Muscarinic receptors are ionotropic or metabotropic ? {{c1::Metabotropic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: All cholinergergic receptors bind to {{c1:: acetylcholine }} while nicotinic receptors can also bind {{c2:: nicotine }} and muscarinic receptors can also bind {{c2:: muscarine }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does the parasympathetic nervous system have cholinergic or adrenergic post-ganglionic receptors ? {{c1::cholinergic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does the sympathetic nervous system have cholinergic or adrenergic post-ganglionic receptors ? {{c1::adrenergic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Adrenergic receptors are ionotropic or metabotropic ? {{c1::metabotropic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: All autonomic neuronal pathways (PSNS/SNS) are composed of what 4 stuctures ? - {{c1::Pre-ganglionic nerve}} - {{c1::Ganglion}} - {{c1::Post-ganglionic nerve}} - {{c1::Target organ}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The parasympathetic nervous system has {{c1::long::short/long}} pre-ganglionic nerve and {{c1::short::short/long}} post-ganglionic nerve
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The sympathetic nervous system has {{c1::short::short/long}} pre-ganglionic nerve and {{c1::long::short/long}} post-ganglionic nerve
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In the sympathetic nervous system pre-ganglionic nerves secrete {{c1::Ach}} and post-ganglionic nerves secrete {{c1::NE (nerves) and Epi/NE (adrenal gland)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In the parasympathetic nervous system pre-ganglionic nerves secrete {{c1::Ach}} and post-ganglionic nerves secrete {{c1::Ach}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: All ganglionic receptors in the SNS and PSNS are of what type ? {{c1::Nicotinic (Cholinergic)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: All target organ receptors in the Sympathetic NS are of what type ? {{c1::Adrenergic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: All target organ receptors in the Parasympathetic NS are of what type ? {{c1::Muscarinic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The synthesis of acetylcholine is catalyzed by which enzyme ? {{c1::Acetyltransferase}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Acetyltransferase takes what two components to make acetylcholine ? - {{c1::Acetyl CoA}} - {{c1::Choline}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which enzyme converts acetylcholine back into acetyl CoA and choline ? {{c1::Acetylcholinesterase}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stimulation of the M2 muscarinic receptor has what effect on the heart ? {{c1::bradycardia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stimulation of the M3 muscarinic receptor acts on which type of muscles ? {{c1::smooth muscles}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The effects of stimulation of the M3 muscarinic receptor can be remembered with what two acronyms ? - {{c1::SLUDS}} - {{c1::BAM}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stimulation of the {{c2::M3}} muscarinic receptor leads to : {{c1::Salivation::S}} {{c2::Lacrimation::L}} {{c3::Urination::U}} {{c4::Defecation::D}} {{c5::Sweating::S}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stimulation of the {{c2::M3}} muscarinic receptor leads to : {{c1::Bronchoconstriction::B}} {{c1::Abdominal cramps::A}} {{c1::Miosis::M}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which category of drugs are non-selective and stimulate all M receptors if administered systemically ? {{c1::M agonists}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Systemic M agonist should be avoided in what 3 broad medical conditions ? - {{c1::Peptic ulcers}} - {{c1::Asthma/COPD}} - {{c1::CHF}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which M agonist medication is used to increase salivary flow in patients with xerostomia ? {{c1::Pilocarpine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How do direct-acting M agonists function ? {{c1::Activates M receptor (mimics Ach)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How do indirect-acting M agonists function ? {{c1::non-competitively inhibit acetylcholinesterase}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Organophosphate insecticides and nerve gases {{c1:: irriversibly::reversibly/irreversibly }} inhibit cholinesterase and poisoning can be treated with {{c2::Pralidoxime}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which M antagonist (antimuscarinic) is used to treat bradycardia in an emergency setting ? {{c1::Atropine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which common stimulant is an N antagonist (ganglionic blocker) that acts by binding to N receptors and can't be removed ? {{c1::Nicotine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which family of drugs are N antagonists that act on the somatic nervous system as neuromuscular blockers ? {{c1::Curares}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Synthesis of (nor)epinephrine : {{c1::Tyrosine}} --> {{c2::L-DOPA}} --> {{c3::Dopamine}} --> {{c4::Norepinephrine}} --> {{c5::Epinephrine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which adrenergic receptors act on smooth muscle ? {{c1::α1, α2, β2 }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which adrenergic receptors act on the heart ? {{c1::β1}}
fields.Extra: β1 and M2 receptors have opposite effect !!
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: α1 receptors have what systemic actions ? - {{c1::Vasoconstriction}} - {{c1::Urinary retention}} - {{c1::Pupil dilation (mydriasis)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: α2 receptors have what systemic actions ? - {{c1::Vasoconstriction}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: β1 receptors have what systemic actions ? - {{c1::Tachycardia}} - {{c1::Renin release from kidneys}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: β2 receptors have what systemic actions ? - {{c1::Bronchodilation}} - {{c1::Vasodilation}} - {{c1::Stops peristalsis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Norepinephrine activates which alpha/beta receptors ? {{c1::all α receptors and β1}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Epinephrine activates which alpha/beta receptors ? {{c1::all α receptors and all β}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Propranolol blocks which alpha/beta receptors ? {{c1::all β receptors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the mechanism of action of amphetamines ? {{c1::stimulates release of stored norepinephrine and dopamine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the mechanism of action of cocaine ? {{c1::inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the mechanism of action of tricyclic antidepressants ? {{c1::inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the mechanism of action of monoamine oxidase inhibitors ? {{c1::blocks enzymatic degradation of monoamines}}
fields.Extra: Monoamines = dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Clonidine and Methyldopa are {{c1::α2}} agonists which block the sympathetic nervous system signal
fields.Extra: Antihypertensives
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The phenomenon where epinephrine actually leads to a vasodilator effect due to the presence of an alpha blocker is called {{c1::epinephrine reversal}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When epinephrine is administered in presence of an alpha blocker , what is the net effect on the vasculature ? {{c1::vasodilation due to beta-2 activation}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When {{c1::norepinephrine}} activates {{c2::baroreceptors}} that stimulate the vagal reflex the result is {{c3::bradycardia}} which can result in vasovagal syncope.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which drug can block the vasovagal reflex ? {{c1::Atropine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The volume of blood ejected from the left ventricle during each contraction is termed the {{c1::stroke volume}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the three parameters that determine blood pressure ? - {{c1::Cardiac output (CO)}} - {{c1::Peripheral resistance (PR)}} - {{c1::Stroke volume (SV)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the formula for mean arterial blood pressure ? {{c1::BP = SV x HR x PR = CO x PR}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The pressure in the arteries when the heart contracts is called the {{c1::systolic pressure}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The pressure in the arteries when the heart relaxes is called the {{c1::diastolic pressure}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The pressure in the ventricles before the heart contracts is termed the {{c1::preload}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The pressure in the arteries against which the ventricles have to pump against is termed the {{c1::afterload}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the general mechanism of action of diuretics ? {{c1::Decrease reabsorption of sodium resulting in net fluid loss}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Furosemide is a {{c1:: loop or high ceiling }} diuretics that acts at the {{c2:: loop of Henle }} of the nephron
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hydrochlorothiazide is a {{c1:: thiazide }} diuretic that acts at the {{c2:: distal tubule }} of the nephron
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Spironolactone is a {{c1:: potassium sparing }} diuretic that acts at the {{c2:: collecting duct }} of the nephron
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Thiazide diuretics can have what effect on potassium levels ? {{c1::decrease --> hypokalemia}}
fields.Extra: May need potassium supplements
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Spironolactone diuretics can have what effect on potassium levels ? {{c1::increase --> hyperkalemia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hydralazine {{c3::opens::opens/closes}} {{c1:: potassium }} channels which causes {{c2:: vasodilation }} and thus a decrease in blood pressure.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Calcium channel blockers act on calcium channels which causes {{c1:: vasodilation }} in order to decrease blood pressure.
fields.Extra: Hyperpolarizes the plasma membrane by not letting Ca2+ in
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the common suffix for calcium channel blockers ? {{c1::-dipine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What enzyme is secreted by the kidneys to increase blood pressure ? {{c1::Renin}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Renin converts {{c1::angiotensinogen}}, which is secreted by the liver , to {{c1::angiotensin I}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which enzyme converts Angiotensin I into Angiotensin II ? {{c1::Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Angiotensinogen is secreted by what organ ? {{c1::liver}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When Angiontensin II agonizes AT1 receptors , what is the systemic result ? {{c1::increased blood pressure}}
fields.Extra: --> Sodium retention and decreased NO release
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Angiontensin converting enzyme (ACE) is mostly produced in the endothelium of which 2 organs ? {{c1::kidneys and lungs}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What effect does Angiotensin II have on the adrenal gland ? {{c1::secretes aldosterone which increases fluid retention}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) have what systemic effect ? {{c1::Decrease blood pressure}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the suffix for ACE inhibitors ? {{c1::-pril}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the suffix for Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARB) ? {{c1::-sartan}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Chest pain caused by insufficient oxygen perfusion to cardiac muscle is termed {{c1::angina}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the antianginal mechanism of action of nitroglycerin ? {{c1::vasodilation of smooth muscle in coronary arteries}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the antianginal mechanism of action of propranolol ? {{c1::Reduces O2 demand by decreasing HR and contractility}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the antianginal mechanism of action of calcium channel blockers ? {{c1::reduce O2 demand by lowering BP }}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Digoxin and Digitalis are examples of anti-CHF drugs from what drug family ? {{c1::cardiac glycosides}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Digoxin and Digitalis function by blocking {{c1:: Na/K ATPase }} which increases the influx of {{c2:: calcium }} and promotes {{c3:: inotropy }} of cardiac muscle cells.
fields.Extra: inotropy = contractility
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which family of antihypertensives is also commonly prescribed to treat congestive heart failure ? {{c1::ACE inhibitors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 1 anti-arrythmics are {{c1::sodium}} channel blockers
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 1a anti-arrythmics {{c1::lengthens::lengthens/shortens}} refractory period to {{c2::slow::slow/speed up}} heartbeat
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 1b anti-arrythmics {{c1::shortens::lengthens/shortens}} refractory period to {{c2::speed up::slow/speed up}} heartrate
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 2 anti-arrythmics are what kind of drugs ? {{c1::beta-blockers}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 3 anti-arrythmics are {{c1::potassium}} channel blockers
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type 4 anti-arrythmics are {{c1::calcium}} channel blockers
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which anti-arrythmic medications can be used to treat atrial fibrillation ? - {{c1::Quinidine}} - {{c1::Verapamil}} - {{c1::Digitalis}}
fields.Extra: Note : patients with A-fib are often on oral anticoagulant therapy
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Schematically, the continuum of CNS excitability goes from {{c1::coma}} to {{c2::seizure}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Broadly, dopamine and serotonin receptors cause the CNS to get more {{c1::excited}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Broadly, GABA receptors cause the CNS to get more {{c1::depressed}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the alternative name for serotonin receptors ? {{c1::5-HT receptors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 1st generation antipsychotics act by blocking which receptors ? {{c1::D2 dopamine receptors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Haloperidol and Phenothiazines are 1st generation antipsychotics and have what side effect if used long term ? {{c1::tardive dyskinesia due to extrapyramidal stimulation}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 2nd generation antipsychotics block which receptors ? {{c1::Dopamine and 5-HT (serotonin) receptors}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What major advantage do 2nd generation antipsychotics have over 1st generation drugs ? {{c1::fewer side effects}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Antidepressant medications function by increasing the amount of {{c1::monoamines}} which excite the CNS
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: SSRIs block the reuptake of {{c1::serotonin}} which leaves more of the molecule to agonize the 5-HT receptors in the synapse
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Tricyclic antidepressants block the reuptake of both {{c1::serotonin}} and {{c1::norepinephrine}} and are also called SNRI
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the mechanism of action of MAOI antidepressants ? {{c1::inhibit breakdown of monoamines}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the drug of choice for treating manic depression (bipolar disorder) ? {{c1::Lithium}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c3::Benzodiazepines and barbiturates}} increase {{c1::GABA}} binding and {{c2::chloride}} ion influx to slow down the CNS
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What family of drugs are ideal for oral sedation in the dental setting ? {{c1::benzodiazepines}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What additive in IV benzodiazepines can induce thrombophlebitis in large veins ? {{c1::propylene glycol}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Propylene glycol in IV benzodiazepines can induce what side effect ? {{c1::thrombophlebitis in large veins}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Do barbiturates or benzodiazepines have more undesirable side effects ? {{c1::barbiturates}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Barbiturates are contraindicated in patients with what blood disorder ? {{c1::intermittent porphyria}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Barbiturate overdose leads to what life-threatening side effect ? {{c1::respiratory depression}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Do barbiturates or benzodiazepines have any analgesic properties ? {{c1::No}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The more soluble a general anesthetic is in blood, the {{c1::more::more/less}} you need to reach critical tension in the brain.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 4 stages of general anesthesia ? Stage I : {{c1::Analgesia/feeling better}} Stage II : {{c2::Delirium}} Stage III : {{c3::Surgical anesthesia}} Stage IV : {{c4::Medullary paralysis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Halothane , a general anesthetic is associated with what type of toxicity ? {{c1::hepatotoxicity}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cell death in the {{c2::substantia nigra}} in Parkinson's disease results in {{c1::dopamine}} deficiency in the brain
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is levodopa used to treat Parkinson's disease instead of dopamine directly ? {{c1::Dopamine can't cross the blood brain barrier but L-DOPA can}}
fields.Extra: L-DOPA is a precursor to dopamine
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Levodopa must be administered with {{c1:: carbidopa }} which blocks {{c2:: DOPA decarboxylase }} from breaking it down before it reaches the brain.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Antibiotic prophylaxis of infective endocarditis is indicated in patients with what conditions ? - {{c1::Prosthetic cardiac valves}} - {{c2::Prothestic material used for cardiac valve repair}} - {{c3::Previous endocarditis}} - {{c4::Congenital heart disease (specific categories)}} - {{c5::Cardiac transplant recipients with cardiac valvulopathy}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation merit antibiotic prophylaxis ? {{c1::No}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cyanotic heart defects involve the passage of blood from the {{c1::right}} side of the heart to the {{c1::left}}
fields.Extra: Deoxygenated blood is distributed to the whole body
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Acyanotic heart defects involve the passage of blood from the {{c1::left}} side of the heart to the {{c1::right}}
fields.Extra: Oxygenated blood is sent back to the lungs, no deoxygenated blood distributed to the circulation
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 5 Ts of cyanotic congenital heart defects ? - {{c1::Tetratology of Fallot}} - {{c2::Truncus arteriosus}} - {{c3::Transposition of great vessels}} - {{c4::Tricuspid atresia}} - {{c5::Total anomalous pulmonary vascular return (TAPVR)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Do atrial or ventricular septal defects require antibiotic prophylaxis ? {{c1::No!}}
fields.Extra: Unless associated with other cardiac problems (eg in Tetralogy of Fallot) pressure in the left heart is high enough to prevent passage of deoxygenated blood
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: For a patient with a prosthetic joint , in what case would you contact the orthopedic surgeon to inquire about antibiotic prophylaxis ? {{c1::history of complications associated with joint replacement surgery}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Antibiotic prophylaxis is reasonable in patients at risk of infective endocarditis before dental procedures involving the manipulation of {{c1::gingival tissue}} or the {{c1::periapical region of the teeth}}, or {{c2::perforation of the oral mucosa}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In HIV patients, when would you prescribe antibiotic prophylaxis ? - {{c1::CD4 < 200}} - {{c1::AIDS related opportunistic infection}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What type of transplant patients need antibiotic prophylaxis ? - {{c1::Bone marrow}} - {{c1::Solid organ (kidney, liver, pancreas, heart, lung)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with neutropenia need antibiotic prophylaxis when neutrophil counts fall below {{c1::< 1000 per microliter of blood}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis taking greater than {{c1::10mg/day}} of Prednisone per day require antibiotic prophylaxis for invasive procedures
fields.Extra: 10mg prednisone = 50mg cortisone = 40mg hydrocortisone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis taking what type of drugs need antibiotic prophylaxis ? {{c1::biological response modifiers}}
fields.Extra: eg. monoclonal antibodies, anti-TNF
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Patients with diabetes need antibiotic prophylaxis when HbA1c > {{c1::8%}} or random blood glucose > {{c1::200mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in an adult : {{c1::2g Amoxicillin 1h before Tx}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in a child : {{c1::50mg/kg Amoxicillin 1h before Tx}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in adults with a penicillin allergy : {{c1::Azithromycin 500mg PO 1h before Tx}}
fields.Extra: eg. monoclonal antibodies, anti-TNF
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in children with a penicillin allergy : {{c1::Azithromycin 15mg/kg PO 1h before Tx}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in children unable to take PO meds : {{c1::Ampicillin 50mg/kg IV 30min before Tx}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: First choice regimen for antibiotic prophylaxis in adults unable to take PO meds : {{c1::Ampicillin 2g IV/IM 30min before Tx}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: If a patient is already taking antibiotics but needs antibiotic prophylaxis for treatment, what do you do ? {{c1::Use another antibiotic class}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: If a patient forgets to take antibiotic prophylaxis before treatment, how long after treatment can antibiotics be administered ? {{c1::2 hours}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What unit is used to measure blood pressure ? {{c1::mmHg}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of hypertension is caused by physical exertion, anxiety, or stress and dissipates after removal of the stimuli ? {{c1::acute hypertension}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of hypertension remains higher than normal with or without stimuli ? {{c1::chronic hypertension}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of acute hypertension manifests only in a health-care setting ? {{c1::white coat hypertension}}
fields.Extra: More prevalent in older populations
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How does a doctor diagnose hypertension ? {{c1::2+ elevated BP readings at 2+ separate occasions}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Normal blood pressure : Systolic {{c1::< 120}} Diastolic {{c1::< 80}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Elevated blood pressure : Systolic {{c1::120-129}} Diastolic {{c1::< 80}}
fields.Extra: Tx : lifestyle modifications
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stage 1 hypertension : Systolic {{c1::130-139}} Diastolic {{c1::80-89}}
fields.Extra: Tx : usually 1 antihypertensive
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stage 2 hypertension : Systolic {{c1::> 140 mmHg}} Diastolic {{c1::> 90 mmHg}}
fields.Extra: Tx : usually 2 antihypertensive meds
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hypertensive crisis : Systolic {{c1::> 180 mmHg}} Diastolic {{c1::> 120 mmHg}}
fields.Extra: Tx : changes in meds, immediate hospitalization if signs of target organ damage
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: List a few risk factors for secondary hypertension : - {{c1::Obesity}} - {{c1::Smoking}} - {{c1::Diet}} - {{c1::Age}} - {{c1::Medication}} - {{c1::Diseases}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 95% of hypertension is termed {{c1::primary/essential}} because there is no identifiable cause
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The manual method of taking a blood pressure is called the {{c1::auscultatory}} method
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The automated method of taking a blood pressure is called the {{c1::oscillometric}} method
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Are wrist cuffs or arm cuffs more accurate for taking blood pressure readings ? {{c1::arm cuffs}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How long should patients rest before taking a blood pressure ? {{c1::5 mins}}
fields.Extra: Seated with feet flat on the floor
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The length of blood pressure cuffs should be about {{c1::80}}% of the arm circumfrence
fields.Extra: Seated with feet flat on the floor
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The width of blood pressure cuffs should be about {{c1::40}}% of the arm circumfrence
fields.Extra: Seated with feet flat on the floor
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What minimum time should you wait before retaking a patient's blood pressure ? {{c1::1-2 minutes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What suffix is often used for alpha-adrenergic blockers ? {{c1::-zosin}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What suffix is often used for alpha and beta blockers ? {{c1::-lol}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What suffix is often used for diuretics ? {{c1::-ide}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with BP > 120/80 mmHg ? - {{c1::Short morning appointments}} - {{c1::Stress management}} - {{c1::Slow chair movements}} - {{c1::Limit epinephrine to 0.04mg}} - {{c1::Avoid epinephrine retraction cord}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with BP > 160/100 mmHg ? - {{c1::Repeat measurement}} - {{c1::Monitor BP during procedure}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with BP > 180/120 mmHg in for elective treatment ? - {{c1::Defer treatment}}
fields.Extra: = hypertensive crisis eg. esthetic composite
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in an asymptomatic patient with BP > 180/120 mmHg in for urgent treatment ? - {{c1::Conservative treatment}} - {{c1::Refer to doctor}}
fields.Extra: = hypertensive urgency eg. incision and drainage, local anesthetic
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in an symptomatic patient with BP > 180/120 mmHg in for urgent treatment ? - {{c1::Immediate referral to ER}}
fields.Extra: = hypertensive emergency eg. incision and drainage, local anesthetic
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A hypertensive crisis (>180/120 mmHg) with symptoms of target organ damage is termed {{c1::hypertensive emergency}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A hypertensive crisis (>180/120 mmHg) without symptoms of target organ damage is termed {{c1::hypertensive urgency}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What symptoms are indicative of hypertensive emergency ? {{c1::Altered mental status, blurred vision, chest pain, dyspnea, peripheral neuropathy, dizziness}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the oral manifestations of hypertension ? {{c1::There aren't any--drugs are the cause}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What side effect can be present in all antihypertensives ? {{c1::Dry mouth/xerostomia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What oral side effect can calcium-channel antihypertensives have ? {{c1::gingival hyperplasia}}
fields.Extra: Gingivectomy may solve problem short term, but high recurrence
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What peri-oral side effects may ACE inhibitors, ARBs, DRI present ? {{c1::Angioedema}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Direct vasodilator antihypertensives may produce what side effect ? {{c1::gingival bleeding}}
fields.Extra: Gingivectomy may solve problem short term, but high recurrence
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 1 indicates a {{c1::normal healthy patient}}
fields.Extra: No special precautions for dental treatment
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 2 indicates a patient with {{c1::mild systemic disease}} with little to no functional limitations.
fields.Extra: Minor treatment modifications
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 3 indicates a patient with {{c1::severe systemic disease}} that limits activity but isn't incapacitating
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 4 indicates a patient with {{c1::severe systemic disease}} that is a constant threat to life
fields.Extra: Elective care is contraindicated, emergency care should be conservative or done in hospital environment
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 5 indicates a patient {{c1::not expected to survive the next 24h}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: ASA 6 indicates a patient {{c1::declared brain-dead patient whose organs are being removed for donor purposes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A current smoker is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A pregnant patient is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with well-controlled hypertension is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with well-controlled diabetes (<7% HbA1c) is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with obesity is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with well-controlled epilepsy is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with asthma is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with asymptomatic thryoid dysfunction is classified as ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Poorly controlled hypertension can be considered as starting at what BP reading ? {{c1::140/90 mmHg}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An obese patient with a BMI of 30 is considered ASA {{c1::2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A morbidly obese patient with a BMI of > 40 is considered ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Morbid obesity is defined as a BMI > {{c1::40}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with stable angina pectoris is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with a history of MI/CVA/TIA > 3 months is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with a ESRD undergoing dialysis is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with alcohol abuse/dependence is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with COPD is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with poorly controlled hypertension is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with poorly controlled diabetes is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with poorly controlled epilepsy is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with symptomatic thyroid dysfunction is classified as ASA {{c1::3}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with unstable angina pectoris is classified as ASA {{c1::4}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with recent MI/CVA/TIA (< 3 months) is classified as ASA {{c1::4}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with ESRD not undergoing dialysis is classified as ASA {{c1::4}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with uncontrolled seizures is classified as ASA {{c1::4}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with septic shock is classified as ASA {{c1::4}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with end-stage cancer or organ dysfunction is classified as ASA {{c1::5}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient with massive trauma is classified as ASA {{c1::5}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Steroids are hormones derived from {{c1::cholesterol}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 4 steroid glands ? - {{c1::Adrenal cortex}} - {{c1::Testes}} - {{c1::Ovaries}} - {{c1::Placenta (during pregnancy)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 types of corticosteroids ? - {{c1::Glucocorticoids (cortisol)}} - {{c1::Mineralocorticoids}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Corticosteroids are secreted by what organ ? {{c1::adrenal cortex of the adrenal gland}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 main effects of cortisol and other glucocorticoids ? - {{c1::Immunosuppression}} - {{c1::Anti-inflammatory}}
fields.Extra: synthetic forms : prednisone, prednisolone, dexamethasone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Aldosterone is what kind of steroid ? {{c1::mineralocorticoid}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 3 major categories of sex steroids ? - {{c1::Progestogens (progesterone)}} - {{c1::Androgens (Testosterone)}} - {{c1::Estrogens (Estradiol)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Cushing's syndrome occurs when the body makes {{c1::too much}} {{c2::cortisol}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which disease is due to overproduction of cortisol ? {{c1::Cushing's syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Primary endogenous Cushing's syndrome is due to an increase of {{c1::cortisol}} secretion at the {{c2::adrenal cortex}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Secondary endogenous Cushing's syndrome is due to an increase of {{c1::ACTH}} secretion at the {{c2::anterior pituitary}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Tertiary endogenous Cushing's syndrome is due to an increase of {{c1::CRH}} secretion at the {{c2::hypothalamus}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Exogenous Cushing's syndrome is due to what ? {{c1::taking too many glucocorticoids}}
fields.Extra: eg. treatment for asthma, RA, excema, ulcerative colitis
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common form of Cushing's syndrome ? {{c1::Exogenous Cushing's syndrome}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What effect does long term corticosteroid use have on the adrenal gland ? {{c1::slows production of cortisol}}
fields.Extra: When a patient stops the meds their body may tak a while to return to previous production of endogenous cortisol
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When is a patient at risk of suppression of adrenal cortex function and adrenal crisis ? {{c1::20mg cortisol for at least 2 weeks in the last 2 years}}
fields.Extra: When a patient stops the meds their body may tak a while to return to previous production of endogenous cortisol
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 20mg hydrocortisone or cortisol is the equivalent of {{c1::5}}mg of Prednisone
fields.Extra: 10mg Prednisone = 40mg Hydrocortisone = 50mg Cortisone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: 20mg hydrocortisone or cortisol is the equivalent of {{c1::0.75}}mg of Dexamethasone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What facial feature is associated with Cushing's syndrome ? {{c1::Moon facies (round, red, full)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What clinical sign manifests on the back of patients with Cushing's syndrome ? {{c1::Buffalo hump}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Addison's disease occurs when the body makes {{c1::too little}} {{c2::cortisol}}
fields.Extra: = ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Primary Addison's disease is due to a decrease of {{c1::cortisol}} secretion at the {{c2::adrenal cortex}}
fields.Extra: = ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Secondary Addison's disease is due to a decrease of {{c1::ACTH}} secretion at the {{c2::anterior pituitary}}
fields.Extra: = ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Tertiary Addison's disease is due to a decrease of {{c1::CRH}} secretion at the {{c2::hypothalamus}}
fields.Extra: = ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What clinical signs of Addison's disease manifest on the skin ? {{c1::Hyperpigmentation such as browning an brown macules}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Acute adrenal insufficiency is also called {{c1::Addisonian crisis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Endogenous Addison's disease is due to what ? {{c1::Immune-mediated destruction of tissue}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What major factor may precipitate an Addisonian crisis in someone with impaired adrenal function ? {{c1::Stress}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A patient in an Addisonian crisis may present what signs and symptoms ? - {{c1::Hypotension}} - {{c1::Vomiting}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What potentially lethal condition can result from an Addisonian crisis ? {{c1::Hypovolemic shock}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What steps should be taken when a patient presents an Addisonian crisis/Acute adrenal insufficiency ? 1. {{c1::Activate EMS}} 2. {{c1::Monitor vital signs}} 3. {{c1::Start IV saline}} 4. {{c1::IV hydrocortisone}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is general anesthesia not recommended for patients with adrenal insufficiency ? {{c1::increases glucocorticoid demand and can make patient susceptible to crisis}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is it important to monitor blood pressure in a patient with Addison's disease or Cushing's syndrome ? {{c1::Hypotension is a sign of acute adrenal insufficiency}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: At what blood pressure reading would you consider fluid replacement or steroid supplementation in a patient with adrenal insufficiency ? {{c1::100/60 mmHg}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In order to prepare for a stressful surgery, what treatment may be indicated in a patient at risk of acute adrenal insufficiency ? {{c1::steroid supplementation (pre-op and maybe post-op)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Diabetes mellitus leads to {{c1::high::low/high}} blood sugar
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Diabetes mellitus is a result of the body's inability to produce or react to {{c1::insulin}} properly
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the role of insulin ? {{c1::Transport sugar from the blood into cells}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Excess glucose in the blood causes what kind of damage to the endothelium ? {{c1::Oxidative damage}}
fields.Extra: damage to endothelium --> atherosclerosis --> hypertension --> MI/stroke/peripheral vascular disease/retinopathy/neuropathy/nephropathy
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 3 P's that represent the most common signs and symptoms of diabetes mellitus ? {{c1::Polydipsia (very thirsty)}} {{c1::Polyphagie (very hungry)}} {{c1::Polyuria (pee alot)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of diabetes is due to the kidney's inability to retain water ? {{c1::Diabetes insipidus}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Diabetes insipidus is due to the body's inability to produce or react to {{c1::ADH (Vasopressin)}} properly
fields.Extra: = anti-diuretic hormone
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the classic signs and symptoms of diabetes insipidus ? {{c1::Polydipsia}} {{c1::Polyuria}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of diabetes begins very early in life ? {{c1::Type I dabetes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type I diabetes is due to insulin {{c1::deficiency}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What treatment for type I diabetes ? {{c1::insulin injections}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In the absence of intracellular glucose as in type I diabetes , cells begin to metabolize fat resulting in the production of {{c1::ketone bodies}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The fruity sweet breath associated with type I diabetes is called {{c1::ketone breath}}
fields.Extra: due to the metabolism of fats and production of ketone bodies
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type I diabetes is due to the LT CD8+ destruction of what cells ? {{c1::beta cells in the pancreatic islets}}
fields.Extra: Responsible for the secretion of insulin
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which type of diabetes begins later in life (adult-onset) ? {{c1::Type II diabetes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Type II diabetes is due to insulin {{c1::resistance}}
fields.Extra: - body can't use insulin properly - target cells don't respond to circulating insulin
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When is supplemental insulin needed in patients with type II diabetes ? {{c1::late-stage cases where beta-cells are "burnt out" from producing so much insulin}}
fields.Extra: body can't use insulin properly
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Diabetes that begins and ends with pregnancy is termed {{c1::gestational diabetes}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 main ways to measure diabetes ? - {{c1::Blood glucose (with a glucometer)}} - {{c1::HbA1c}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The results given by a glucometer are measured in what units ? {{c1::mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is a glucometer not the ideal method of assessing diabetes stability ? {{c1::blood glucose varies throughout the day}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An HbA1c test measures the amount of {{c1::glycosylated hemoglobin}} found in red blood cells
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why are HbA1c levels able to reflect the blood glucose level over the past 3 months ? {{c1::lifespan of RBC is about 120 days}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: HbA1c used for diabetes status diagnosis : Diabetes : {{c1:: > 6.5 %}} Prediabetes : {{c1::5.7-6.4 %}} Normal : {{c1:: < 5.7 %}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the target HbA1c value for a well-controlled diabetes patient ? {{c1::< 7%}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: At what HbA1c level does a patient get classified as poorly controlled diabetes ? {{c1::> 8%}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: To evaluate a patient's diabetes status, how recent must their HbA1c test be ? {{c1::within the past 3 months}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A fasting blood sugar test involves not eating or drinking (except water) for at least {{c1::8}} hours before the test
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An oral glucose tolerance test involves taking a blood sugar test {{c1::2}} hours after a meal (prostprandial)
fields.Extra: Either 2h after a meal OR 8h fast then sugar intake then test after 2h
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Fasting blood sugar test : Diabetes : {{c1::> 126 mg/dL}} {{c2::Impaired fasting glucose}} : {{c1::100-125 mg/dL}} Normal : {{c1::< 99 mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Glucose tolerance test : Diabetes : {{c1::> 200 mg/dL}} {{c2::Impaired glucose tolerance}} : {{c1::140-199 mg/dL}} Normal : {{c1::< 139 mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are a few examples of rapid-acting insulin ? {{c1::Lispro}} {{c1::Aspart}} {{c1::Glulisine}}
fields.Extra: Stronger and faster acting, but shorter half-life than human insulin
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is an example of an intermediate-acting insulin ? {{c1::Neutral Protamine Hagedorn (NPH)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are some examples of a long-acting insulin ? {{c1::Detemir}} {{c1::Glargine}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A typical diabetes type I prescription would include 1 {{c1::rapid-acting insulin}} before meals and 1 {{c1::long-acting insulin}} per day
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Sulfonylurea antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::Increase insulin secretion by beta cells}}
fields.Extra: Taken 30 mins before a meal Don't combine with NSAIDs
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Biguanide antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::Decrease glucose production}}
fields.Extra: Taken with meals - Decrease neoglucogenesis - Decrease glucose uptake from intestins - Decrease glucagon production
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Dipeptidyl Peptidase-4 inhibitor antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::Indirectly increase insulin production}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Thiazolidinedione antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::Improve insulin sensitivity by producing more GLUTs}}
fields.Extra: Taken with meals GLUT = glucose transporters
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitor antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::decrease glucose level by delaying carbohydrate breakdown in the gut}}
fields.Extra: Taken just before meals
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: SGLT2 inhibitor antidiabetics have what effect ? {{c1::Decrease circulating glucose by increasing urine excretion of glucose instead of reabsorbing it}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hypoglycemia is defined as a blood sugar below {{c1::70 mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a conscious patient with hypoglycemia , what do you do ? {{c1::Administer sugar (orange juice/glucose tab)}} {{c1::Wait 15 mins and test blood sugar}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In an unconscious patient with hypoglycemia , what do you do ? {{c1::Activate EMS}} {{c1::IV dextrose or IM glucagon}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The acronym TIRED can be used to identify hypoglycemic patients {{c1::Tachycardia::T}} {{c2::Irritability::I}} {{c3::Restlessness::R}} {{c4::Excessive hunger::E}} {{c5::Diaphoresis::D}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Hyperglycemia is defined as a fasting blood sugar >= {{c1::126 mg/dL}} or post-prandial blood sugar of >= {{c2::200 mg/dL}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What life-threatening complication may result from hyperglycemia ? {{c1::Diabetic ketoacidosis}} {{c1::Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The acronym SINFUL can be used to identify hyperglycemic patients {{c1::Sweet breath (ketone breath)::S}} {{c2::Increased thirst (Polydipsia)::I}} {{c3::Nausea and vomiting::N}} {{c4::Frequent::F}} {{c4::Urination (polyuria)::U}} {{c5::Loss of clear vision::L}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which class of drugs should be avoided in patients taking sulfonylureas because of the increased risk of hypoglycemia ? {{c1::NSAIDs}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which 2 types of drugs should be avoided in diabetes patients because of the increased risk of hyperglycemia ? {{c1::Glucocorticoids}} {{c1::Levofloxacin (fluoroquinolones)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with well controlled diabetes ? - {{c1::Short AM appointments}} - {{c1::Normal insulin an meals prior to appointment}} - {{c1::Use glucometer before treatment}} - {{c1::Have glucose source available}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with poorly controlled diabetes in for elective treatment ? {{c1::Defer treatment}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with poorly controlled diabetes in for urgent treatment and asymptomatic for diabetic complications ? {{c1::Remove infection}} {{c1::Referral to PCP}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions should be taken in a patient with poorly controlled diabetes in for urgent treatment and symptomatic for diabetic complications ? {{c1::Immediate referral to ER}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What oral manifestations of diabetes are the result of dry mouth/xerostomia ? - {{c1::Caries (more glucose in saliva too)}} - {{c1::Oral candidiasis}} - {{c1::Burning mouth}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What oral manifestations of diabetes are the result of peripheral vascular damage ? - {{c1::Delayed wound healing}} - {{c1::Increased incidence and severity of infections}} - {{c1::Gingivitis and periodontal disease}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The tooth to which a bridge is attached is called the {{c1::abutment}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a bridge, the crown that attaches to the abutment is called the {{c1::retainer}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a bridge, the fake tooth is called the {{c1::pontic}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In a bridge, the element that joins the retainer to the pontic is termed the {{c1::connector}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What amount of bone support carries a poor prognosis for a bridge ? {{c1::half or less bone support}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Posterior distal single retainer {{c1::cantilever}} bridges carry a poor prognosis because the forces of mastication are too great.
fields.Extra: Anterior mesial cantilevers are more often used and have a better prognosis
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does increasing the number of splinted teeth improve or worsen the the prognosis of a bridge ? {{c1::Worsens}}
fields.Extra: Differential movement and partial unseating of crowns can cause secondary caries
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Do nonrigid or rigid bridge connectors carry a worse prognosis ? {{c1::nonrigid}}
fields.Extra: Only used in specific situations
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In long span bridges the inclusion of what intermediary element carries a relatively poor prognosis ? {{c1::pier/intermediary abutment}}
fields.Extra: Only used in specific situations
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why are endodontically treated teeth "weaker" ? {{c1::Removed dentin makes them weaker}}
fields.Extra: Nothing to do with being nonvital. Less structure = less resistance.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Compromised {{c1::endodontically treated }} teeth and {{c1::periodontal}} teeth should not be used as bridge abutments.
fields.Extra: Nothing to do with being nonvital. Less structure = less resistance.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the ideal crown-to-root ratio for an abutment tooth ? {{c1::1:2}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the minimum crown-to-root ratio for an abutment tooth ? {{c1::1:1}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which rule of thumb recommends that the PDL surface of abutment teeth be greater than or equal to the imaginary PDL surface of the missing teeth ? {{c1::Ante's law}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When would splinting teeth in a bridge be recommended ? {{c1::Periodontal surface of abutment tooth insufficient to support the bridge}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Splinting of teeth in a bridge distributes {{c1::occlusal forces}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When replacing a canine, which teeth should be splinted together and why ? {{c1::central and lateral incisor to avoid lateral drifting}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which root shape is preferable as an abutment tooth ? {{c1::Divergent::divergent/conical}} {{c1::Multiple::single/multiple}} {{c1::Curved::curved/straight}} {{c1::Broad::narrow/broad}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 4 broad indications for a removable partial denture ? - {{c1::Distal extension}} - {{c2::Long span}} - {{c3::Bone loss around potential abutments}} - {{c4::Bridge or implant is too expensive}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: A denture used to replace all teeth in an arch is called a {{c1::complete denture}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Complete dentures are contraindicated in the maxilla when only the {{c1::mandibular anterior teeth}} are present because severe damage to the premaxilla occurs.
