fields.Text: Who first publicized the use of acid etching to enable enamel bonding ? {{c1::Buonocore (1955)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Is bonding to dentin or enamel more predictable ? {{c1::Enamel}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does dentin bonding get more or less predictable as you get closer to the pulp ? {{c1::less predicatble (larger tubules, more water)}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why does dentin bonding get less predictable as you get closer to the pulp ? {{c1::larger tubules (greater density of tubules), more water from pulp}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The debris from cutting dental substrate is called the {{c1::smear layer}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What length of etching is usually recommended for enamel ? {{c1::30 seconds}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What length of etching is usually recommended for dentin ? {{c1::15 seconds}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Acid etching of tooth substance is usually done with {{c1::30-40% orthophosphoric acid}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How can you visually distinguish sufficiently etched enamel ? {{c1::Chalky/frosty white appearance}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the purpose of etching enamel ? {{c1::Create microporosities}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the purpose of etching dentin ? {{c1::Remove the smear layer and widen dentinal tubules}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The primer component of an adhesive system is composed of {{c1:: monomers }} and {{c2:: solvents }} which serve to infiltrate dentinal tubules and displace water
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Primer from an adhesive system may cause {{c1::contact dermatitis}} when it contacts skin
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which component of an adhesive system can cause contact dermatitis ? {{c1::Primer}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the roles of primer in an adhesive system ? - {{c1::Infiltrate enamel prisms/dentinal tubules}} - {{c1::Prevent collagen collapse}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common type of monomer used in bond/adhesive ? {{c1::Bis-GMA}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the role of bond in an adhesive protocol ? {{c1::Adhere to the primer and overlying composite}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is HEMA monomer used in the primer component of adhesive systems ? {{c1::It's amphiphillic so it can better infiltrate hydrophillic environments}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The mechanical interface between tooth and adhesive it called the {{c1::hybrid layer}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Adhesive that infiltrates dentinal tubules and etched enamel forms structures called {{c1::resin tags}} that allow micromechanical retention
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What kind of retention composite restorations rely on ? {{c1::micromechanical retention}}
fields.Extra: Relies on the large amount of friction from between the resin tags and dental surface
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is some kind of acid etching required on cut dentin before applying primer/adhesive ? {{c1::Smear layer must be removed to allow resin infiltration}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why is some kind of acid etching required on cut dentin before applying primer/adhesive ? {{c1::Smear layer must be removed to allow resin infiltration}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which part of the bonding interface is indicated by [1] ? {{c1::Composite resin}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which part of the bonding interface is indicated by [2] ? {{c1::Adhesive resin}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which part of the bonding interface is indicated by [3] ? {{c1::Hybrid layer}}
fields.Extra: = Mix of adhesive resin and primer in the collagen matrix
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which part of the bonding interface is indicated by [4] ? {{c1::Resin tags}}
fields.Extra: = Mix of adhesive resin and primer in the collagen matrix
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which part of the bonding interface is indicated by [5] ? {{c1::Dentin}}
fields.Extra: = Mix of adhesive resin and primer in the collagen matrix
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 2 main families of enamel-dentin bonding system ? - {{c1::Etch and rinse}} - {{c1::Self-etch systems}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 3 major components of any enamel-dentin bonding system ? - {{c1::Acid}} - {{c1::Primer}} - {{c1::Adhesive}}
fields.Extra: All 3 components are always present, but they may be combined with another step
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What components of the bonding system are present in the bottle of a 2 step etch-and-rinse adhesive ? {{c1::Primer + Adhesive}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What components of the bonding system are present in the first bottle of a 2 step self-etch adhesive ? {{c1::Acid + Primer}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What components of the bonding system are present in the second bottle of a 2 step self-etch adhesive ? {{c1::Adhesive}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What components of the bonding system are present in a bottle of 1 step self-etch adhesive ? {{c1::Acid + Primer + Adhesive}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What type of resin matrix is found in most composites ? {{c1::Bis-GMA}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the filler particles of most composites made of ? {{c1::Quartz/silica}}
fields.Extra: Lots of different types (eg. barium silicate, silicon dioxide, zirconia-silica, zirconium oxide)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which component of composite resin confers its radiopacity ? {{c1::Filler particles}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the coupling agent used to promote adhesion between the resin matrix and the filler particles in composite ? {{c1::Silane}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: {{c1::Nano}}-fill composites have the best optical qualities and greatest polishability
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Do smaller or larger filler particles result in greater mechanical properties of composites ? {{c1::Larger filler particles}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does less or more filler particles result in less water absorption in composites ? {{c1::more filler particles leads to less absorption}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which component of self-cure composites serves as the initiator ? {{c1::Benzoyl peroxide}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which component of self-cure composites serves as the activator ? {{c1::Tertiary amines}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the most common photoinitiator in most light-cure composites ? {{c1::Camphorquinone}}
fields.Extra: More and more are being developped and not all lights provide the right wavelengths
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The maximum absorbance of camphorquinone is seen at what wavelength ? {{c1::468 nm}}
fields.Extra: More and more are being developped and not all lights provide the right wavelengths
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Most composites when they polymerize undergo shrinkage of about {{c1::2-3}}%
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The {{c1:: C-factor }} is the ratio of {{c2:: bonded }} to {{c2:: not bonded }} surfaces in an adhesive restoration.
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why are high C-factors problematic for adhesive restorations ? {{c1::lead to greater microleakage}}
fields.Extra: - As the composite polymerizes it pulls on the sides of the preparation - If the force of polymerization is greater than the strength of the bond then you get microleakage
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: To counter the polymerization shrinkage associated with conventional composites, each added layer should be no more than {{c1::2}}mm
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What kind of retention does glass ionomer restorations rely on ? {{c1::chemical bonding for retention}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Glass ionomer cements are a combination of {{c1::polyacrylic acid::acid}} and {{c1::fluoroaluminosilicate glass (FAS)::base}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How do glass ionomers chemically bond to teeth ? {{c1::Polyacrylic acid chelates calcium in dental structures}}
fields.Extra: = Same mechanism as how it sets
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Glass ionomers release which mineral ? {{c1::Fluoride}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: How do resin-modified glass ionomers (RMGI) set ? {{c1::acid-base reaction AND free radical polymerization}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the other name for polyacid-modified resin composites ? {{c1::Compomers}}
fields.Extra: Looked very promising but disappointing clinically so not very used
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What level of pain should be expected from a few simple extractions, SRP, routine endodontics, or gingivectomy ? {{c1::Mild pain::Mild/Moderate/Severe pain}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What level of pain should be expected from a surgical extraction, apicoectomy, implant surgery or quadrant flap surgery with bony resection ? {{c1::Moderate pain::Mild/Moderate/Severe pain}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What level of pain should be expected from a bony impaction extractions or complex periodontal or implant surgery ? {{c1::Severe pain::Mild/Moderate/Severe pain}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When mild postprocedural pain is anticipated, what is the recommended analgesic therapy? {{c1::Ibuprofen 200-400mg every 4-6h as needed}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When moderate postprocedural pain is anticipated, what is the recommended analgesic therapy? {{c1::Ibuprofen 400-600mg + APAP 500mg every 6h for 24h then Same regimen as needed every 4-6h}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When severe postprocedural pain is anticipated, what is the recommended analgesic therapy? {{c1::Ibuprofen 400-600mg + APAP 500mg + Hydrocodone 10mg for 24-48h then Ibuprofen 400-600mg + APAP 500mg as needed every 4-6h}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the safest analgesic to prescribe during pregnancy? {{c1::Acetaminophen}}
fields.Extra: NSAIDs only during 1st and 2nd trimester Opioids generally contraindicated
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why should asthmatic patients be given APAP instead of NSAIDs ? {{c1::NSAIDs can trigger bronchospasm}}
fields.Extra: NSAIDs can exacerbate asthma symptoms Opioids cause repiratory depression
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why should asthmatic patients be given APAP instead of opioids ? {{c1::Opioids cause respiratory depression}}
fields.Extra: NSAIDs can exacerbate asthma symptoms Opioids cause repiratory depression
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What analgesic therapy should be given for patients with liver disease/cirrhosis ? {{c1::Low dose acetaminophen (2g/24h)}}
fields.Extra: Half-life of liver-metabolized drugs is significantly prolonged in these patients so a lower dose must be administered
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why analgesic dose modification needed in patients with liver disease/cirrhosis ? {{c1::Half-life of liver-metabolized drugs is significantly prolonged}}
fields.Extra: APAP 2g/24h (500mg qid)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Generally, what analgesic therapy should be prescribed for patients treated for hypertension or ischemic heart disease ? {{c1::Acetaminophen}}
fields.Extra: - NSAIDs interact with some antihypertensives and can cause an increase in BP - Patients treated with anticoagulants or anti-platelets should not be prescribed NSAIDs due to bleeding risk
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What analgesic therapy should be prescribed for patients with gastric ulcers ? {{c1::Acetaminophen}}
fields.Extra: NSAIDs interfere with the stomach's ability to protect itself from gastric acid
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What analgesic therapy should be prescribed for patients with chronic kidney disease ? {{c1::Acetaminophen}}
fields.Extra: NSAIDs are associated with acute kidney injury and progression of chronic kidey disease
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Why isn't the use systemic antibiotics not usually recommended in the treatment of an acute apical abscess ? {{c1::antiotics don't get distributed well to necrotic/dying tissue}}
fields.Extra: Systemic antibiotics are useful for preventing infections with systemic involvement (fascial space or lymph node involvement, fever, or malaise)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: If immediate dental care isn't available, what pharmacological treatment should be prescribed for symptomatic apical periodontitis ? {{c1::Analgesics}}
fields.Extra: APAP (500/1000mg) + Ibuprofen (400-600mg) Systemic antibiotics are useful for preventing infections with systemic involvement (fascial space or lymph node involvement, fever, or malaise) Source : https://jada.ada.org/article/S0002-8177(19)30617-8/fulltext?dgcid=PromoSpots_EBDsite_ABX
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: If immediate dental care isn't available, what pharmacological treatment should be prescribed for symptomatic irreversible puplitis ? {{c1::Analgesics}}
fields.Extra: APAP (500/1000mg) + Ibuprofen (400-600mg) Systemic antibiotics are useful for preventing infections with systemic involvement (fascial space or lymph node involvement, fever, or malaise) Source : https://jada.ada.org/article/S0002-8177(19)30617-8/fulltext?dgcid=PromoSpots_EBDsite_ABX
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: In the absence of possible dental treatment, what pharmacological treatment should be prescribed for an acute apical abscess ? {{c1::Antibiotics + analgesics}}
fields.Extra: Amoxicillin 500mg every 8h for 7 days Azithromycin 500mg (day 1) then 250mg for 4 days Systemic antibiotics are useful for preventing infections with systemic involvement (fascial space or lymph node involvement, fever, or malaise) Source : https://jada.ada.org/article/S0002-8177(19)30617-8/fulltext?dgcid=PromoSpots_EBDsite_ABX
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: When might antibiotics be prescribed, even if immediate dental care is available ? {{c1::Immunocompromised patient}} {{c1::Significant swelling (fascial space)}} {{c1::Systemic involvement (fever, malaise, lymphoadenopathy)}}
fields.Extra: Amoxicillin 500mg every 8h for 7 days Azithromycin 500mg (day 1) then 250mg for 4 days Systemic antibiotics are useful for preventing infections with systemic involvement (fascial space or lymph node involvement, fever, or malaise) Source : https://jada.ada.org/article/S0002-8177(19)30617-8/fulltext?dgcid=PromoSpots_EBDsite_ABX
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: For infections that do not respond promptly to Amoxicillin or Azithromycin , what second-line alternatives exist? {{c1::Change to Augmentin (Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid 500/125mg tid}} {{c1::Add Metronidazole (500mg tid)}}
fields.Extra: Source : https://jada.ada.org/article/S0002-8177(19)30617-8/fulltext?dgcid=PromoSpots_EBDsite_ABX
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste for Booster Prep

fields.Text: HIV is a {{c1::enveloped::enveloped/non-enveloped}} {{c2::RNA::DNA/RNA}} retrovirus.
fields.Extra: HIV is a retrovirus because it uses a reverse transcriptase to transform RNA into DNA Image courtesy : Daniel Beyer, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: HIV infects cells with which {{c1:: CD4 }} receptors, which are found in {{c2:: T helper cells}} and other immune cells
fields.Extra: CD4 found in monocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells and T helper cells Image courtesy : Daniel Beyer, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: HIV is found in every bodily fluid except {{c1::saliva}} and {{c1::sweat}}
fields.Extra: Present in amniotic fluid, blood, semen, tears, cerebrospinal fluid, urine However, blood and semen are the only at-risk fluids for transmission
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What are the 3 modes of transmission of HIV? 1. {{c1::Sexual transmission}} 2. {{c1::Exposure to blood}} 3. {{c1::Vertical transmission}}
fields.Extra: Sexual transmission : Anal sex (+++), Vaginal sex (++), Oral sex (+) Blood exposure : Needle-stick (0.3% chance of acquiring), shared IV drug use Vertical transmission : during pregnancy, dleivery, or through breastmilk (~25% likelihood of passing on HIV if untreated)
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stage 1 HIV is defined as CD4+/mm 3 > {{c1::500}}
fields.Extra: Right after infection : test positive, but mostly asymptomatic Image courtesy : Splette, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stage 2 HIV is defined as CD4+/mm 3 between {{c1::200}} and {{c1::499}}
fields.Extra: Symptoms evolve : lymphoadenopathy, weight loss, fever, diarrhea, oral candiasis Image courtesy : Splette, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Stage 3 HIV (AIDS) is defined as CD4+/mm 3 <{{c1::200}}
fields.Extra: Symptoms worsen : kaposi sarcoma, wasting, opportunistic infections (oral candidiasis, Epstein-Barr virus, odontogenic infections) Image courtesy : Splette, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What is the main screening test used to diagnose HIV ? {{c1::ELISA}}
fields.Extra: Usually, positive diagnosis necessitates : 2 positive ELISAs then a Western blot or PCR to confirm Image courtesy : Cavitri, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What class of drugs are used to treat HIV ? {{c1::Antiretrovirals}}
fields.Extra: They target different parts of the HIV lifecycle Image courtesy : Jmarchn, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: The use of multiple types of antiretroviral therapies to treat HIV is termed {{c1::Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART)}}
fields.Extra: They target different parts of the HIV lifecycle Image courtesy : Jmarchn, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions for HIV patients with CD4+/mm 3 > 200 ? {{c1::None, same as any other patient}}
fields.Extra: Have patient bring latest complete blood count and medication list
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions for HIV patients with CD4+/mm 3 < 200 for non-invasive care ? {{c1::None, same as any other patient}}
fields.Extra: Have patient bring latest complete blood count and medication list
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What precautions for HIV patients with CD4+/mm 3 < 200 for invasive (surgical) care ? - {{c1::Consult with patient's physician and obtain complete blood count}} - {{c1::Antibiotic prophylaxis}}
fields.Extra: Complete blood count is needed to assess neutrophils, CD4, and platelets
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Which uncommon malignant oral lesion is often identified when an HIV patient transitions into AIDS ? {{c1::Kaposi sarcoma}}
fields.Extra: Purple nodular lesions often found on the gingiva or the palate. Due to HHV-8 infection Image courtesy : Sol Silverman, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What periodontal lesions may occur in severely immunocompromised AIDS patients ? {{c1::necrotizing gingivitis/periodontitis}}
fields.Extra: Painful +++, "decapitated" papilla, horrible halitosis Image courtesy : Dr. Mohamed Hamze, CC BY-SA 3.0
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: What white lesions may be seen in HIV/AIDS patients? - {{c1::Oral candidiasis +++}} - {{c1::Oral hairy leukoplakia}}
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: Does a patient legally have to disclose their HIV status? {{c1::No}}
fields.Extra: Except in Arkansas
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep

fields.Text: An implant must be placed at least {{c1::5}} mm away from the mental foramen
fields.Source: Made by thefrenchdentiste and adapted for Booster Prep
